findings.title: embeddings_handler missing ensure_model_inference guard — inference grant bypass
findings.issue: embeddings_handler does not call auth_scope.access_policy().ensure_model_inference(&model) before forwarding to the upstream provider. chat_completions_handler (line 134), anthropic_messages_create_handler (line 148), and gemini_action_handler (line 213) all received the guard in this diff; embeddings was missed. The policy check is the only layer enforcing per-model inference grants for ApiToken and grant-bound ExternalApp principals.
findings.failure_scenario: Token with ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["allowed-model"] } POSTs to /v1/embeddings with model "restricted-model". find_alias succeeds and the request is forwarded upstream — 200 embedding response returned. Same token calling /v1/chat/completions with the same non-granted model correctly receives 403.
findings.recommendation: Add auth_scope.access_policy().ensure_model_inference(&model)?; immediately after extracting model from the request in embeddings_handler, before find_alias is called. Add unit tests parallel to test_chat_completions_scoped_token_forbidden and test_chat_completions_external_app_model_forbidden covering both ApiToken and ExternalApp restricted cases.
findings.rationale: All three other inference surfaces in the same diff were guarded; the embeddings handler in the same file was not. The grant system's contract — Specific ids = deny all others — is violated for this endpoint.
findings.evidence: git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD shows ensure_model_inference was added only to chat_completions_handler (routes_oai_chat.rs:134), anthropic_messages_create_handler (routes_anthropic.rs:148), and gemini_action_handler (routes_gemini.rs:213). The diff has zero lines touching embeddings_handler. In the current source, embeddings_handler (line 250) extracts `model` at line 255 and calls find_alias at line 258–262 with no intervening ensure_model_inference call. grep -n "ensure_model_inference" routes_oai_chat.rs returns only line 134 (inside chat_completions_handler).
findings.verify_notes: The omission is unambiguous — three sibling handlers in the same diff received the guard; embeddings_handler in the same file did not. The failure scenario is fully reachable: any ApiToken or ExternalApp principal with a ModelGrant::Specific restriction can call POST /v1/embeddings with a non-granted model and receive a 200 embedding response, while the same credential is correctly rejected on /v1/chat/completions. Critical priority is appropriate because it is a complete bypass of the per-model grant enforcement for a live inference endpoint.

findings.title: retain_listable_models uses prefix string for forward_all_with_prefix aliases instead of per-model IDs
findings.issue: For API aliases where forward_all_with_prefix = true, retain_listable_models calls keep(a.prefix.as_deref().unwrap_or("")) — judging the entire alias by the prefix string alone. The UI picker (grantItems.ts) generates grant item IDs as prefix + model.id (e.g. "gemini-flash" when prefix is ""), which is what OAI listing and inference enforcement also use. The Bodhi /bodhi/v1/models listing therefore uses a different ID namespace than every other enforcement site, making specific-model grants on forward_all aliases non-functional for that endpoint.
findings.failure_scenario: User creates a Gemini API alias with forward_all_with_prefix=true and empty prefix. Creates an API token with ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["gemini-flash"] } (the ID the picker shows). GET /bodhi/v1/models → retain_listable_models calls keep("") → model_listable("") = false → alias pruned entirely. GET /v1/models (OAI) → per-entry check model_listable("gemini-flash") → true → alias shown. POST /v1/chat/completions → ensure_model_inference("gemini-flash") → true → succeeds. Inference and OAI discovery work but the Bodhi models list is broken for this token.
findings.recommendation: For forward_all_with_prefix aliases, iterate the models array and call keep(&format!("{}{}", prefix, m.id())) for each entry (returning true if any passes), matching the per-model-ID logic used by OAI listing and matchable_models(). Update or add unit tests to cover the corrected branch.
findings.rationale: grantableModelItems (new in this diff) and retain_listable_models (new in this diff) were added together but use different ID forms for this alias type, guaranteeing a broken grant for any user who uses a forward_all Gemini or OpenAI alias with specific-model grants from the UI.
findings.evidence:
Both `retain_listable_models` (services/src/models/model_objs.rs) and `grantableModelItems` (bodhi/src/lib/grantItems.ts) were introduced in this diff. The bug is at lines 1008–1009 of model_objs.rs:

```rust
if a.forward_all_with_prefix {
  return keep(a.prefix.as_deref().unwrap_or(""));   // BUG: uses prefix, not per-model IDs
}
```

The three sites use different IDs for the same entity:
- **UI picker** (`grantItems.ts` lines 13–16): iterates `alias.models`, generates `"${prefix}${model.id}"` (e.g., `"gemini-flash"` for prefix="" and model.id="gemini-flash"). This is what ends up in `ModelGrant::Specific { ids: [...] }`.
- **OAI `/v1/models`** (`routes_oai_models.rs` line 75): calls `api_alias.matchable_models()`, which regardless of `forward_all_with_prefix` iterates `self.models` and emits `prefix + model.id` strings, then filters via `policy.model_listable(&m.id)` using those full IDs.
- **Bodhi `/bodhi/v1/models`** (`routes_models.rs` line 147): calls `alias.retain_listable_models(|id| policy.model_listable(id))`. For `forward_all_with_prefix = true`, the closure receives only the raw prefix string (`""` for empty prefix), not per-model IDs. `model_listable("")` checks `ids.contains("")` → `false`.

Consequently, with `ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["gemini-flash"] }` on a `forward_all_with_prefix = true` alias with empty prefix: `keep("")` → `false` → entire alias is pruned from `/bodhi/v1/models`, while `/v1/models` correctly retains "gemini-flash". No unit tests cover `retain_listable_models` with the `forward_all_with_prefix = true` branch at all.
findings.verify_notes: The doc comment on `retain_listable_models` explicitly states "a `forward_all_with_prefix` API alias matches an unbounded set and is judged by `keep(prefix)` as a whole" — this was the *intended* design, but it is internally inconsistent with how the UI and the OAI listing expand the same aliases. Since both conflicting functions (retain_listable_models and grantableModelItems) were introduced together in this diff, neither was pre-existing; the finding correctly identifies a genuine correctness bug. Priority "important" is appropriate: the bug breaks the Bodhi models list endpoint for a specific combination (forward_all_with_prefix alias + specific model grant), while inference (OAI chat) and OAI discovery still work correctly.

findings.title: update_revocation and list_approved_for_user have no repository-level unit tests or cross-tenant isolation tests
findings.location: entire file — update_revocation and list_approved_for_user absent
findings.issue: Both new DefaultDbService methods introduced in this diff — update_revocation and list_approved_for_user — have zero coverage in test_access_request_repository.rs. Repository tests cover create, get, update_approval, update_denial, update_failure, get_by_access_request_scope, but not these two. Additionally, test_access_request_repository_isolation.rs (72 lines) was not modified; the cross-tenant isolation tests required by the codebase convention for all new tenant-scoped DB mutations are absent. Critical paths untested: (a) double-revoke of already-Revoked record; (b) list_approved_for_user excludes Revoked/Draft; (c) tenant isolation in update_revocation (the explicit TenantId filter defending SQLite); (d) wrong-user check returning ItemNotFound; (e) non-Approved status guard.
findings.failure_scenario: A regression removes the TenantId filter from update_revocation. User A in tenant A calls POST /access-requests/<id>/revoke where <id> belongs to user A in tenant B. The request succeeds, revoking a cross-tenant grant. No repository-level test exists to catch this.
findings.recommendation: Add rstest/sqlx cases to test_access_request_repository.rs: (1) list_approved_for_user returns only Approved rows, excludes Revoked/Draft; (2) update_revocation happy path Approved→Revoked; (3) update_revocation wrong user → ItemNotFound; (4) update_revocation on non-Approved → AccessRequestNotDraft (or the renamed variant from F7); (5) update_revocation on a different tenant's record → ItemNotFound. Add test_cross_tenant_revoke_blocked and test_cross_tenant_list_approved_isolation to the isolation file. Use #[values("sqlite", "postgres")] on all cases.
findings.rationale: The revocation path is security-critical: it is the kill-switch for app tokens. The crates/CLAUDE.md convention requires isolation tests for all new tenant-scoped DB operations. The two new methods are invoked by security-critical routes.
findings.sources: test:backend (update_revocation), test:backend (list_approved_for_user), sec:lifecycle, bug:services, prior:services-review F1
findings.evidence:
Confirmed by reading the actual files and running git diff:

1. `/crates/services/src/app_access_requests/test_access_request_repository.rs` (363 lines) contains zero test functions referencing `update_revocation` or `list_approved_for_user`. Grep across all test_*.rs files returns no hits for these method names in services.

2. `git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD -- test_access_request_repository.rs test_access_request_repository_isolation.rs` produced empty output — neither file was modified in this diff. The isolation file (73 lines) only contains `test_cross_tenant_app_access_request_isolation` which tests the `get` path only.

3. Route-level tests in `crates/routes_app/src/apps/test_access_request.rs` DO exist and use a real `DefaultDbService` (not mocked via `AppServiceStubBuilder.with_db_service()`). They cover: happy-path Approved→Revoked transition, wrong-user rejection, and non-approved (Draft) rejection. But `build_test_harness` calls `builder.with_tenant(services::Tenant::test_default())` seeding only TEST_TENANT_ID — no multi-tenant setup, so cross-tenant isolation for revocation is untested at any layer.

4. Both `update_revocation` and `list_approved_for_user` are confirmed new in this diff (appear in `access_request_repository.rs` diff under `+` lines at lines 39-82 of the diff hunk).
findings.verify_notes: The finding accurately identifies the gap. The route-level tests (routes_app) do exercise the real DB and cover wrong-user and non-approved-status rejection paths, which partially mitigates the "zero coverage" claim — so this is not as dire as the finding implies for those specific scenarios. However, the cross-tenant isolation scenario remains genuinely uncovered at every layer: no repository test, no isolation test, and the route tests only seed a single tenant. The TenantId filter in `update_revocation` (line 388 of the diff: `.filter(app_access_request_entity::Column::TenantId.eq(&tenant_id_owned))`) is the SQLite defense-in-depth guard, and its removal would go undetected. Priority stays "important" rather than "critical" because the route tests do provide partial integration coverage and the actual DB query does contain the filter; this is a test coverage gap rather than a missing implementation guard.

findings.title: retain_listable_models has no unit tests despite non-trivial branching
findings.issue: New method with four branches (User, Model, ModelRouter — single-model keep — and Api with forward_all_with_prefix sub-cases including in-place models.retain and empty-list drop signal) has zero services-level unit tests. The route handler test test_oai_models_scoped_token_filters_listing uses only User/Model aliases from the data fixture; the Api branch with prefix pruning is never exercised.
findings.failure_scenario: A scoped token with ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["openai/gpt-4o"] } lists models when an ApiAlias has prefix="openai/" and models=[gpt-4o, gpt-3.5-turbo]. retain_listable_models should keep gpt-4o and drop gpt-3.5-turbo. If the retain call were accidentally written as a.models.retain(|m| !keep(...)) (negated), both models are dropped and the alias is removed. No test catches this.
findings.recommendation: Add inline unit tests in model_objs.rs covering: (1) Api with forward_all_with_prefix=false, some models kept → returns true with pruned list; (2) all models filtered → returns false; (3) forward_all_with_prefix=true judged by prefix; (4) User/Model/ModelRouter pass-through. A route-handler test with a real ApiAlias + scoped token would also strengthen coverage.
findings.rationale: The only non-trivial branch mutates state (in-place models.retain). The drop-on-empty contract is consumed by retain_mut at routes_app/src/models/routes_models.rs:147. Correctness cannot be inferred from other branches.
findings.evidence: 1. `retain_listable_models` is introduced in this diff at `crates/services/src/models/model_objs.rs:1002`. The `Alias::Api` branch does in-place `a.models.retain(|m| keep(&format!("{}{}", prefix, m.id())))` and returns `!a.models.is_empty()` to signal the drop-on-empty contract. 2. `test_model_objs.rs` was not modified in this diff and has no occurrence of `retain_listable_models` — confirmed by grep returning empty output. 3. The route-level test `test_oai_models_scoped_token_filters_listing` (added in this diff in `crates/routes_app/src/oai/test_models.rs`) uses the `app()` fixture, which calls `with_data_service()` → `seed_test_user_aliases()`. That function seeds only three `UserAlias` rows (llama3, testalias_exists, tinyllama) — no `ApiAlias` entries. So only the `Alias::User(a) => keep(&a.alias)` branch of `retain_listable_models` is exercised; the `Alias::Api` branch (mutating retain + empty-list drop) is never reached by any test.
findings.verify_notes: The method implementation looks correct as written — the retain condition is not negated, and the prefix concatenation is sound. The gap is a test-coverage issue: a future refactor (e.g. negating the closure, changing prefix formatting, or altering the empty-list contract) would go undetected. The drop-on-empty return value is consumed by `retain_mut` at `routes_app/src/models/routes_models.rs:147`, making this contract load-bearing. Priority stays at important rather than critical because the current code is not defective — the risk is undetected future regression in the Api+prefix branch.

findings.title: Anthropic and Gemini model-listing handlers bypass model-listable grant filter
findings.issue: This diff added ensure_model_inference to the Anthropic and Gemini inference handlers but not to their corresponding model-listing handlers. All four listing handlers iterate all aliases without calling access_policy().model_listable(id), so a restricted API token sees every configured model. By contrast, oai_models_handler (same diff, line 90) correctly filters with models.retain(|m| policy.model_listable(&m.id)).
findings.failure_scenario: Token with ModelGrant::Specific { ids: [] } (no inference rights) calls GET /anthropic/v1/models. The handler returns all Anthropic model entries, revealing which Anthropic providers and aliased model names are configured. The same token calling GET /v1/models (OAI) receives an empty list due to the filter added in this diff.
findings.recommendation: In each of the four listing handlers, compute the policy after building the model list and retain only entries for which policy.model_listable(prefixed_model_id) is true, using the same prefixed-ID form used by OAI listing. In the get handlers, add a not-listable → 404 guard mirroring oai_model_handler line 141.
findings.rationale: The diff established a uniform filter-listings-by-grant pattern across OAI and Bodhi endpoints. The pattern was not applied to the provider-specific listing endpoints that are in API-token-accessible route groups (anthropic_apis and gemini_apis both accept TokenScope::User).
findings.evidence: The diff (4dea5ea9..HEAD) added `model_listable` filtering to OAI endpoints in `crates/routes_app/src/oai/routes_oai_models.rs` at lines 89-90 (`models.retain(|m| policy.model_listable(&m.id))`) and line 141 (`if !auth_scope.access_policy().model_listable(&id)`), and added `ensure_model_inference` guards to Anthropic (line 148 of routes_anthropic.rs) and Gemini (line 213 of routes_gemini.rs) inference handlers. However, the four listing/get handlers were not touched by the diff and contain NO `access_policy()` or `model_listable` calls: `anthropic_models_list_handler` (routes_anthropic.rs:184-226), `anthropic_models_get_handler` (routes_anthropic.rs:229-249), `gemini_models_list` (routes_gemini.rs:123-146), and `gemini_models_get` (routes_gemini.rs:149-174). Both route groups accept `TokenScope::User` (routes.rs:257 and ~282) so API tokens reach these handlers. A token with `ModelGrant::Specific { ids: [] }` calling `GET /anthropic/v1/models` receives the full model list while the same token on `GET /v1/models` gets an empty filtered list — an inconsistency introduced by this diff's partial application of the new policy enforcement pattern.
findings.verify_notes: The finding accurately identifies an asymmetry in this diff: the listing-filter pattern was added to OAI but not to the Anthropic/Gemini listing handlers. Inference is blocked on all three providers (the `ensure_model_inference` guard was added uniformly), so the impact is information disclosure — revealing configured Anthropic/Gemini provider names and alias prefixes to tokens with no inference rights — not actual inference bypass. This keeps the severity at "important" rather than "critical".

findings.title: Unbound ExternalApp resolves to Unrestricted (all models + all MCPs) on model inference paths
findings.issue: An ExternalApp context with grants: None resolves to AccessPolicy::Unrestricted — full model and MCP access — on /v1/chat/completions, /anthropic/*, Gemini, and /bodhi/v1/models. Only the apps_apis route group rejects access_request_id: None. The security gain relies on a KC-exchanged ExternalApp token never being minted without a scope_access_request: scope, but this invariant is undocumented near the code. If reachable, an unbound ExternalApp has broader access than any specific grant would allow.
findings.failure_scenario: An ExternalApp token is minted (or a bug in token_service allows one through) without an access_request scope. AccessPolicy::of wildcard arm returns Unrestricted. The token calls /v1/chat/completions with any model and succeeds, bypassing all grant enforcement.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) confirm via code search that KC-exchanged ExternalApp tokens cannot exist without a scope_access_request: claim (handle_external_client_token :242-310) and document this invariant as a comment near AccessPolicy::of; or (b) change the wildcard arm to treat ExternalApp { grants: None } as Grants(&ApprovedResourcesV1::default()) (deny-MCP, all-models by current default) so MCP at minimum fails closed and the code is self-defending.
findings.rationale: Defense-in-depth: enforcement should not depend on an unstated minting invariant for the broadest-access principal. The current code silently permits all inference if the upstream guard ever changes.
findings.evidence: Traced the full call chain: (1) token_service.rs `handle_external_client_token` sets `validated_record = None` when no `scope_access_request` claim is present, making `role = None` and `grants = None`; (2) `api_auth_middleware` (apis/api_middleware.rs line 71) has `AuthContext::ExternalApp { role: None, .. } => Err(ApiAuthError::MissingAuth)` — this pre-existing, unchanged arm blocks any ExternalApp-without-scope token from reaching inference handlers; AccessPolicy::of is never reached for the stated failure scenario. The `role: Some, grants: None` case is structurally possible only if a DB row has `approved_role` set but `approved` NULL, which cannot be achieved through `update_approval` (access_request_repository.rs lines 184-195 sets them atomically). The wildcard arm behavior is documented in the module comment of token_grants.rs lines 11-12 as intentional pre-grants backward compatibility.
findings.verify_notes: The finding's stated failure scenario ("ExternalApp token minted without access_request scope → Unrestricted on inference routes") is blocked by api_auth_middleware's role:None guard, which the finding overlooked. The finding's claim that "only apps_apis rejects access_request_id: None" is wrong — api_auth_middleware provides the equivalent gate on inference routes via the role:None → MissingAuth arm. The deeper defense-in-depth concern (ExternalApp { role: Some, grants: None } silently getting Unrestricted) is real but requires a corrupt/tampered DB row, since update_approval always writes approved and approved_role together. The module-level comment acknowledges the Unrestricted wildcard as intentional pre-grants behavior. The recommendation to add a doc comment documenting the role↔grants co-invariant near AccessPolicy::of is a low-cost improvement but not urgent.

findings.title: Apps self-select whether models can be restricted — owner has no UI to clamp models for an app that did not request the control
findings.location: ApprovedResourcesV1 Default impl ~line 281; consent crates/bodhi/src/routes/apps/access-requests/review/index.tsx lines 270-274 and 333
findings.issue: ModelGrant::default() = All and ApprovedResourcesV1::default().models_access = All. The consent screen's AI-Models section only renders when the app set models_list || models_access in its request. If the app omits both flags, the approve payload forces models_access: {type:'all'} and the owner has no UI affordance to restrict. The party being restricted unilaterally decides restrictability. MCP already does the right thing (default empty Specific); models is the opposite.
findings.failure_scenario: An over-broad app omits models_list and models_access from its access request. The consent screen renders without the model-restriction UI. The owner approves. The app receives all models — identical to the pre-effort behaviour that the grant system was designed to fix.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) always render the model-restrict GrantBlock at consent regardless of the app's requested flags (defaulting to All for continuity), giving the owner a ceiling they can lower; or (b) document this as an accepted design decision in docs/architecture/security.md so it is not mistaken for default-deny. Make models_access default symmetric with mcps_access (empty Specific) if the intent is restrictive-by-default.
findings.rationale: Owner consent should be a hard ceiling, not gated by the requester's self-declared flags. The effort's headline goal ('apps can now be restricted') is only partially met — restriction requires the app to opt in, leaving legacy or poorly-written apps with full model access.
findings.sources: prior:cross-cutting-review F4, prior:routes_app-review F1, prior:services-review F5
findings.evidence:
All four code paths described in the finding are present in the committed diff:

1. `crates/services/src/grants/grant_objs.rs` line 9: `#[default]` is on `All` — `ModelGrant::default() = All`.

2. `crates/services/src/app_access_requests/access_request_objs.rs` line 281: `models_access: ModelGrant::default()` — `ApprovedResourcesV1::default().models_access = All`.

3. `crates/bodhi/src/routes/apps/access-requests/review/index.tsx` line 333: `{(req.models_list || req.models_access) && (` — the entire AI-Models GrantBlock section (including the restriction controls) is conditional on the app-requested flags being set. When the app omits both, the section is not rendered.

4. `index.tsx` lines 270-274: the approve payload builder unconditionally emits `{ type: 'all' }` in the else branch: `models_access: req.models_access ? (modelMode === 'all' ? { type: 'all' } : { type: 'specific', ids: models }) : { type: 'all' }`.

5. The asymmetry with MCPs is real and explicitly coded differently: `index.tsx` lines 288-292 emit `{ type: 'specific', ids: [] }` in the MCP else branch. The Rust comment at line 265-266 of `access_request_objs.rs` even reads: "Owner-granted MCP instances beyond the by-url requests. Defaults to none (empty `Specific`) — unlike a token's all-access default." The author deliberately restricted MCPs but not models.

6. `docs/architecture/security.md` has no entry for this model-access default — no accepted-risk documentation exists.
findings.verify_notes: The design choice is intentional and explicitly commented in the source: "If the app didn't request a model selector, the owner can't restrict — the app keeps all-model access (the pre-grants default)" (index.tsx lines 268-269). This was a deliberate backward-compatibility decision. However, it is not documented in security.md as an accepted risk, and it structurally defeats the owner-ceiling property the grant system was built to provide: any app that omits `models_access: true` from its request descriptor opts out of model restrictions entirely, without the owner having any UI affordance to override. The MCPs path (which correctly defaults to empty Specific) shows the pattern for a restrictive default is known and available. Priority stays "important" rather than "critical" because the net effect equals the pre-existing no-grants behaviour (all models accessible), so no new capability is introduced for apps that already had access — but the consent promise is broken for new apps that intentionally or inadvertently omit the flag.

findings.title: AccessRequestNotDraft reused as revoke status guard — wrong error code surfaces to clients
findings.issue: DbError::AccessRequestNotDraft was created for the approve/deny path (Draft→Approved/Denied transition). In the newly-added update_revocation the same variant guards the Approved→Revoked transition. The generated error code is db_error-access_request_not_draft (HTTP 409). Semantically the guard means 'the request must be in Approved state to be revoked' but the code name reads 'the request is not in Draft state', which is the wrong semantic. The three existing uses of this variant at the approve/deny paths (lines 178, 242, 306) are semantically correct.
findings.failure_scenario: Caller invokes POST /access-requests/{id}/revoke on an already-revoked grant. Response body carries code db_error-access_request_not_draft and message containing 'revoked ... cannot be updated'. A client switching on the error code to display a human-readable reason interprets this as 'the request was never submitted (not in Draft)' rather than 'already revoked'.
findings.recommendation: Add a new DbError variant: AccessRequestNotRevocable { id: String, status: String } with error_meta Conflict, error message 'Access request {id} has status {status} and can only be revoked when Approved.' Use it at lines 407-411 in update_revocation only.
findings.rationale: Error codes are the machine-readable contract for client error handling. Using the wrong variant encodes the wrong precondition for callers who branch on error codes programmatically.
findings.evidence: At `/Users/amir36/Documents/workspace/src/github.com/BodhiSearch/BodhiApp/crates/services/src/app_access_requests/access_request_repository.rs:407-411`, the newly-added `update_revocation` guards `row.status != AppAccessRequestStatus::Approved` and returns `DbError::AccessRequestNotDraft { id, status }`. This exact line was added in this diff (`git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD` shows `+            return Err(DbError::AccessRequestNotDraft {`). The variant definition in `crates/services/src/db/error.rs:64-66` has error message "Access request '{id}' has status '{status}' and cannot be updated." — the variant name encodes the wrong precondition (`NotDraft` rather than `NotApproved`/`NotRevocable`). The generated error code will be `db_error-access_request_not_draft`. The test at `routes_app/src/apps/test_access_request.rs:343-368` (`test_revoke_non_approved_request_rejected`) only asserts `assert_ne!(StatusCode::OK, response.status())` — it does not assert the specific error code. No current frontend code in `crates/bodhi/src/` switches on `error.code`; `useAppAccessRequests.ts:97` reads only `error?.response?.data?.error?.message`.
findings.verify_notes: The defect is real: `AccessRequestNotDraft` is semantically correct for approve/deny paths (lines 178, 242, 306 guard Draft state) but wrong for the revocation path (line 408 guards Approved state). The error code `db_error-access_request_not_draft` communicates the wrong precondition to any API client that branches on machine-readable codes. However, impact is currently low because: (1) no existing frontend or test code switches on this specific error code — only the human-readable message is consumed; (2) the message text "has status '{status}' and cannot be updated" does convey the actual status, just not the correct expected state. Priority is downgraded from important to nice-to-have because the defect is purely a semantic/naming issue in the error contract with no currently reachable misbehavior for existing callers. The recommendation to add `AccessRequestNotRevocable` is the correct fix.

findings.title: HTTP verb mismatch: approve endpoint uses PUT while deny and revoke use POST
findings.issue: The three owner-facing state-transition actions on an access request use inconsistent HTTP verbs: approve is PUT (utoipa and Axum route binding), while deny and revoke are POST. All three are one-shot non-idempotent transitions (a second call returns 409 Conflict), so none satisfies the RFC definition of idempotency that would justify PUT.
findings.failure_scenario: A client infers the approve verb from the sibling deny/revoke POST endpoints and sends POST /bodhi/v1/access-requests/{id}/approve → 405 Method Not Allowed. Auto-generated SDKs driven by OpenAPI also diverge in method name between approve (PUT) and deny/revoke (POST).
findings.recommendation: Change approve to POST to match deny and revoke: update the utoipa annotation from put to post; change put(apps_approve_access_request) to post(apps_approve_access_request) in routes.rs. All three state-transition action endpoints on the same resource family should use the same verb.
findings.rationale: REST convention: POST is correct for non-idempotent action endpoints that change state. Consumers and auto-generated SDKs derive the verb from the OpenAPI spec, so the mismatch creates a correctness hazard for any client handling these actions uniformly.
findings.evidence: routes_apps.rs line 227: `#[utoipa::path(put, ...)]` for approve; routes.rs line 354: `put(apps_approve_access_request)`. Deny uses `post` (routes_apps.rs line 376, routes.rs line 358). Revoke (new in this diff) uses `post` (routes_apps.rs line 440, routes.rs line 364). Auth test in test_access_request_auth.rs line 9 explicitly encodes `("PUT", "/bodhi/v1/access-requests/test-id/approve")`, confirming the PUT is intentional. The git diff shows revoke was newly added with POST, deepening the existing approve-PUT vs deny-POST inconsistency.
findings.verify_notes: The inconsistency is real and confirmed. The `approve` endpoint predates this diff (already used PUT), and the newly added `revoke` endpoint uses POST, making three sibling action endpoints: approve=PUT, deny=POST, revoke=POST. A client that infers the approve verb from the deny/revoke pattern would send POST to /approve and receive 405 Method Not Allowed. Priority "important" is correct — it is a real API correctness hazard for SDK consumers and for any client uniformly handling these action endpoints, but not critical since the approve endpoint does function correctly when called with PUT and the OpenAPI spec accurately reflects the divergence.

findings.title: GET /bodhi/v1/user reflects effective grants at different JSON depths for ApiToken vs ExternalApp
findings.issue: Effective grant information is at different paths depending on principal type. For ApiToken: models and mcps are at the root level (flattened from TokenInfo). For ExternalApp: models and mcps are nested inside the optional 'access' sidecar field. No field explicitly identifies an ExternalApp vs a browser session (only presence of 'access' hints at this). A consumer reading root.models gets the correct answer for API tokens but misses the ExternalApp grant entirely.
findings.failure_scenario: A proxy service calls GET /bodhi/v1/user and reads response.models to determine what models the caller can access. For an ApiToken caller this works correctly. For an ExternalApp caller the field is absent at root level (undefined); the actual grant is at response.access.models. The proxy incorrectly concludes the ExternalApp has unrestricted access.
findings.recommendation: Document the asymmetry explicitly in the utoipa description for UserInfoEnvelope: state that 'access' is present only for ExternalApp principals and ApiToken principals embed grants directly in the root object. Consider whether a future API version should unify grant location (always emit 'access' for any restricted principal so consumers check one path). Ensure TypeScript client consumers use a discriminant function that checks both locations.
findings.rationale: API consumers building grant-aware code must branch on auth_status to find the correct JSON path. The OpenAPI schema marks 'access' as optional without documenting when it appears vs is absent, forcing client authors to read source code.
findings.evidence: The asymmetry is confirmed in source: `crates/routes_app/src/users/routes_users_info.rs` lines 118-132 put `models`/`mcps` into `TokenInfo` which is `#[serde(flatten)]`-merged to envelope root for `auth_status:"api_token"` responses. Lines 155-172 put grants into `access: Option<ResourceAccessInfo>` nested under the envelope for `auth_status:"logged_in"` ExternalApp responses. Tests in `test_user_info.rs` explicitly assert this shape: ApiToken test asserts `access: None` with root-level `models`/`mcps`; ExternalApp test asserts `access: Some(ResourceAccessInfo { models: ..., mcps: ... })`. The `access` field doc says "present only for external-app principals". The `auth_status` serde tag (`#[serde(tag = "auth_status")]` on `UserResponse`) is the existing discriminant between the two shapes.
findings.verify_notes: The asymmetry is real and newly introduced in this diff, so "plausible" is justified over "refuted". However, priority "important" is too high: (1) `auth_status` is the proper discriminant — a consumer that checks `auth_status == "api_token"` before reading root `models`/`mcps` is correct; (2) for `auth_status == "logged_in"` there is never a root `models` field regardless of principal type (Session/ExternalApp share this variant), so no naive consumer reading `response.models` would conclude "unrestricted access" — they would get `undefined` for ALL logged_in principals; (3) the TypeScript client generated from OpenAPI has the correct discriminated union types, so typed consumers handle both shapes; (4) `access` has explicit doc "present only for external-app principals". The real gap is OpenAPI documentation not clearly stating that ApiToken grants appear at root while ExternalApp grants appear in `access`, requiring cross-variant documentation rather than a code fix. Priority should be "nice-to-have".

findings.title: Missing handler-level grant enforcement tests for Anthropic, Gemini, MCP proxy, and mcps_show
findings.location: anthropic routes_anthropic.rs:148; gemini test_gemini_routes.rs / routes_gemini.rs:213; mcps/mcp_proxy.rs:70; mcps/routes_mcps.rs:87-88
findings.issue: Four new ensure_model_inference / ensure_mcp_connect / mcp_listable calls added in this diff have no handler-level 403/404 tests. (1) anthropic_messages_create_handler calls ensure_model_inference at line 148 — test_anthropic_messages.rs has no restricted-grant 403 test; (2) gemini_action_handler calls ensure_model_inference at line 213 — test_gemini_routes.rs has no restricted-grant 403 test; (3) mcp_proxy_handler calls ensure_mcp_connect at line 70 — no test file exists for the proxy handler at all; (4) mcps_show returns synthetic 404 for not-listable MCPs at lines 87-88 — no test covers this early-return branch. Removing any of these four guards would break no CI test.
findings.failure_scenario: An API token with ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["claude-3-opus"] } calls POST /anthropic/v1/messages with model 'claude-3-sonnet'. Expected: 403. A regression silently removing the guard at line 148 would pass all current tests. Same pattern for Gemini inference, MCP proxy, and mcps_show existence leak.
findings.recommendation: Add to test_anthropic_messages.rs: test_anthropic_messages_scoped_token_forbidden (ApiToken with Specific mismatched) and test_anthropic_messages_external_app_model_forbidden. Add equivalent to test_gemini_routes.rs. Create test_mcp_proxy.rs (or extend test_mcps.rs) with ensure_mcp_connect forbidden case (mock McpService.get to return a valid entity; expect 403 without upstream call). Add to test_mcps.rs: GET /mcps/{id} with a scoped token lacking the grant → 404 and mock never called.
findings.rationale: The OAI handler and test_token_grants.rs prove the AccessPolicy logic is correct, but they do not exercise the wiring in the other handler files. Test coverage for each handler is the regression net specifically for that handler's enforcement call.
findings.sources: test:backend (Anthropic), test:backend (Gemini), test:backend (MCP proxy), test:backend (mcps_show), prior:routes_app-review F6
findings.evidence: 1. routes_anthropic.rs:148 adds `auth_scope.access_policy().ensure_model_inference(&model)?;` in this diff. grep for `grant|ModelGrant|forbidden|TokenGrant` in test_anthropic_messages.rs returns zero hits; its 8 test functions cover only forwarding/header scenarios. 2. routes_gemini.rs:213 adds the same call; test_gemini_routes.rs has 15+ tests with zero hits on the same grep. 3. mcp_proxy.rs:70 adds `auth_scope.access_policy().ensure_mcp_connect(&id)?;`; `ls mcps/` shows no test_mcp_proxy.rs at all. 4. routes_mcps.rs:83-88 adds the `!mcp_listable` early-return 404 for mcps_show; test_mcps.rs has no test function exercising GET /mcps/{id}. Contrast: oai/test_chat_completions.rs has `test_chat_completions_scoped_token_forbidden` (line 102) and `test_chat_completions_external_app_model_forbidden` (line 153) for the identical wiring pattern. The access_policy logic is unit-tested in shared/test_token_grants.rs but the handler-level wiring is not tested for these four callsites.
findings.verify_notes: The finding is accurate on all four points. The AccessPolicy logic itself is well-tested in test_token_grants.rs, so this is a wiring-coverage gap rather than a logic defect. Deleting any of the four new guard lines would silently pass CI, making this a real regression-net hole. Priority "important" is correct: not "critical" because the policy logic is sound and deliberate removal would be needed, but not "nice-to-have" because the OAI handler already establishes the pattern and the other handler tests are conspicuously missing it.

findings.title: models_access/mcps_access: bool in RequestedResourcesV1 but ModelGrant/McpGrant in ApprovedResourcesV1 under the same field names
findings.location: RequestedResourcesV1.models_access (line ~234, bool) and ApprovedResourcesV1.models_access (line ~255, ModelGrant); same for mcps_access
findings.issue: The field names models_access and mcps_access are identical in both structs but hold completely different types: bool (UI-driver flag) in the request vs ModelGrant/McpGrant (actual grant object) in the approved body. This is reflected verbatim in openapi.json and ts-client. Any non-TS client (Python, Rust, curl) that reads requested.models_access and naively copies it to approved.models_access will send true (bool) where a {type:'all'} or {type:'specific',ids:[...]} object is required.
findings.failure_scenario: A Python client reads GET /apps/access-requests/:id/review → requested.models_access = true. Constructs PUT body as {"approved": {"models_access": true, ...}}. Rust backend attempts to deserialize true as a tagged ModelGrant enum → 400/422 deserialization error, silently failing the approval.
findings.recommendation: Rename the request booleans to break the collision: RequestedResourcesV1.models_access → show_models_access (or want_models_access), mcps_access → show_mcps_access. Update openapi.json, ts-client, frontend, and E2E fixtures. Alternatively version the RequestedResources envelope (V2) if external clients already use the old names.
findings.rationale: Same field name with different JSON types in sibling schemas that appear together in the same API flow is a classic client trap. The Rust type system and TypeScript prevent it internally, but generated or hand-rolled clients in any other language see only the field name.
findings.evidence: Git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD introduces both structs in crates/services/src/app_access_requests/access_request_objs.rs: RequestedResourcesV1.models_access is `pub models_access: bool` (line 240) and RequestedResourcesV1.mcps_access is `pub mcps_access: bool` (line 246), while ApprovedResourcesV1.models_access is `pub models_access: ModelGrant` (line 260) and ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps_access is `pub mcps_access: McpGrant` (line 268). OpenAPI spec confirms: RequestedResourcesV1.models_access = {type: boolean}, ApprovedResourcesV1.models_access = {$ref: ModelGrant} (a oneOf of {type:"all"} or {type:"specific",ids:[...]}). Same collision for mcps_access. Both structs are in the same diff (new code). The failure scenario is exact — submitting {"approved": {"models_access": true}} against the approval endpoint would fail deserialization because the backend expects a ModelGrant tagged enum, not a boolean.
findings.verify_notes: The collision is real and in new code introduced in this diff. The doc comments in both structs acknowledge the mirroring intentionally ("Field names mirror RequestedResourcesV1") but this actually makes the problem worse — the deliberate naming symmetry amplifies the client trap. Priority stays "important": no security or data-loss risk (the 400 error is immediate and loud), but it is a genuine external API design inconsistency that will confuse any non-TypeScript client author reading the field names without carefully checking the schema types.

findings.title: mcps field name collision: McpGrant in TokenGrantsV1 vs Vec<McpApproval> in ApprovedResourcesV1
findings.issue: Both TokenGrantsV1 and ApprovedResourcesV1 expose a JSON field named mcps, but with completely unrelated types: McpGrant ({type:'all'} or {type:'specific',ids:[...]}) in the API-token envelope vs Vec<McpApproval> (array of {url, status, instance?} consent records) in the app-approval envelope. In ts-client this manifests as TokenGrantsV1.mcps?: McpGrant vs ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps?: Array<McpApproval>.
findings.failure_scenario: A developer writes a generic function getMcpGrant(envelope) that accesses envelope.mcps, intending to check whether a grant covers a given MCP id. For a TokenGrantsV1 this yields a McpGrant with an allows(id) check. When accidentally passed an ApprovedResourcesV1, mcps is an array of approval records — mcps.allows is not a function. TypeScript catches this only if both types are in scope; a JS or OpenAPI-generated client silently produces wrong values.
findings.recommendation: Rename ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps to mcps_by_url (or mcp_approvals) to distinguish the url-consent list from the grant-style mcps field. Update the Rust struct, serde rename attribute, openapi.json, ts-client, consent review handler, and all callers. It is a breaking change to the approve request body shape.
findings.rationale: The collision surfaced because mcps is the natural shorthand for two different things in the two envelopes. With the domain-first renaming from commit 8844c135 the gap widened: ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps_access now clearly names the extra grant, but the legacy mcps array kept its bare name while TokenGrantsV1.mcps is the main grant field.
findings.evidence: Both fields confirmed in current source: `TokenGrantsV1.mcps: McpGrant` (token_objs.rs:42) and `ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps: Vec<McpApproval>` (access_request_objs.rs:264). Both pre-dated 4dea5ea9 (verified via `git show 4dea5ea9`). The diff added `ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps_access: McpGrant` (absent at 4dea5ea9 — no output from grep), which is a McpGrant alongside the pre-existing Vec<McpApproval> mcps field, slightly compounding naming confusion. TypeScript generated types confirm the structural collision: `ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps?: Array<McpApproval>` vs `TokenGrantsV1.mcps?: McpGrant` (ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts lines 348, 1731). Rust doc on TokenGrantsV1 says "Intentionally standalone — NOT shared with the App-access-request envelope; the two may diverge."
findings.verify_notes: The naming collision is real but pre-existing — the diff range did not introduce the TokenGrantsV1.mcps vs ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps collision; it only added mcps_access:McpGrant alongside the existing mcps:Vec<McpApproval> in ApprovedResourcesV1. TypeScript prevents the described failure scenario in correctly-typed code: a function parameterized as `TokenGrantsV1 | ApprovedResourcesV1` would have `mcps` typed as `McpGrant | Array<McpApproval>` and the structural difference catches misuse at compile time. The failure requires deliberately untyped JavaScript or duck-typed generic code, which is contrived for this strict-TypeScript codebase. This is a legitimate API naming inconsistency worth tracking as a future cleanup but does not meet the bar for "important" — no runtime defect, no silent wrong value in the current code, and the Rust doc comments explicitly explain the intended distinction.

findings.title: Apps mutation hooks use inline error extraction instead of shared extractErrorMessage, dropping network-error details
findings.location: useRevokeAppAccess line 97, useDenyAppAccessRequest line 121, useApproveAppAccessRequest line 64
findings.issue: The three new/modified mutations extract error messages inline (error?.response?.data?.error?.message || 'Failed to ...') instead of calling extractErrorMessage(error, 'Failed to ...') as the tokens hooks do. The inline version only reads the Bodhi API envelope. On a network failure (CORS block, timeout, server down) where error.response is undefined, the inline version swallows the underlying AxiosError message and always shows the hardcoded fallback.
findings.failure_scenario: User is on the App Tokens screen and the backend is unreachable. They click Revoke Access and confirm. Network call fails with AxiosError whose .message is 'Network Error'. The hook fires onError('Failed to revoke app access') instead of 'Network Error', hiding the real cause from the toast.
findings.recommendation: Import and call extractErrorMessage(error, 'Failed to ...') in place of the three inline expressions, matching the pattern in useTokens.ts.
findings.rationale: useTokens.ts already uses extractErrorMessage consistently. Diverging from the shared utility in newly added hooks increases the surface area where error messages are degraded.
findings.verify_notes: The finding is confirmed but its scope is partially overstated: only useRevokeAppAccess (line 97) is new in this diff; useApproveAppAccessRequest and useDenyAppAccessRequest carried the same inline pattern before 4dea5ea9 and were not modified here. The behavior gap is real — on network failures the newly added hook returns 'Failed to revoke app access' instead of the underlying 'Network Error'. Priority is downgraded from important to nice-to-have because the error is still shown to the user (just less specifically), and this is a UX/debuggability issue with no correctness or security consequence.

findings.title: grantableModelItems misclassifies ModelRouterResponse aliases as 'local' in the access picker
findings.issue: The structural narrowing else if ('alias' in alias) matches not only UserAliasResponse and ModelAliasResponse (real local GGUF aliases) but also ModelRouterResponse (a composite routing alias), because ModelRouterResponse also carries an alias string field. ModelRouterResponse items are assigned type: 'local' and land in the 'Local Models' group in the picker panel with a 'local' badge.
findings.failure_scenario: A user creates a ModelRouter alias 'my-router'. On the New Token or review-consent screen, the model picker panel shows 'my-router' under 'Local Models' with a 'local' badge alongside real local-file aliases. If the user applies a 'Local only' type filter, 'my-router' incorrectly appears. If they apply 'API only', it is correctly excluded — but it is a valid grant target they would miss. The grant ID (alias.alias) sent in the payload is still correct; enforcement works. The bug is display-only but misleads user intent.
findings.recommendation: Add a structural guard before the 'alias' branch to detect ModelRouterResponse (e.g. 'targets' in alias && 'strategy' in alias) and emit an item with no type field, so it renders in the untyped/ungrouped bucket rather than 'Local Models'. Alternatively use the source field if it is reliably present on all AliasResponse variants.
findings.rationale: The models list endpoint includes all four AliasResponse variants. The check 'alias' in alias is too broad — it matches ModelRouterResponse, ModelAliasResponse, and UserAliasResponse alike. Only ApiAliasResponse is correctly discriminated.
findings.evidence: Traced the full type hierarchy and rendering path. In ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts line 1227-1235, ModelRouterResponse is defined with fields: source, id, alias: string, targets, strategy, created_at, updated_at. It has alias but NOT models or prefix. In grantItems.ts (new file, entirely in diff), the narrowing at line 12 (`'models' in alias && 'prefix' in alias`) only catches ApiAliasResponse; the fallthrough at line 18 (`else if ('alias' in alias)`) matches ModelRouterResponse, ModelAliasResponse, and UserAliasResponse alike — all receive `type: 'local'`. In AccessPickerPanel.tsx, `const local = filtered.filter((i) => i.type === 'local')` (line 53) lands ModelRouter items in the "Local Models" group (line 56), they receive a `local` badge (line 120), and with the "API only" type filter active, `i.type !== typeFilter` excludes them (line 45), making valid ModelRouter grant targets invisible to users who filter by API.
findings.verify_notes: The defect is real and fully traceable: ModelRouterResponse.alias satisfies 'alias' in alias, so it is misclassified as local. Enforcement is unaffected (the alias.alias value used as grant id is correct), but the picker mis-groups and, more importantly, the type filter silently hides ModelRouter aliases when "API only" is selected, causing users to miss them as valid grant targets. Priority "important" is correctly rated — it is a display+UX bug rather than a security or data-integrity flaw, but it can cause users to misconfigure grants by excluding valid options.

findings.title: Wrong CardTitle 'New App Token' on the API Token creation page
findings.issue: The diff changed <CardTitle>New API Token</CardTitle> to <CardTitle>New App Token</CardTitle> on the /tokens/new/ route which creates a user-issued API Token (via TokenForm/useCreateToken). The breadcrumb on line 22 still correctly reads 'New API Token'. The page has contradictory labels. A genuinely separate 'App Tokens' management screen now exists at /tokens/apps/ for third-party app grants, making the conflation directly harmful to information architecture.
findings.failure_scenario: A user navigating to /tokens/new/ to create an API token sees the breadcrumb 'New API Token' but the card heading 'New App Token', confusing the two fundamentally different token types.
findings.recommendation: Revert the CardTitle to 'New API Token' to match the breadcrumb and the route's purpose.
findings.rationale: The title was correct before this diff. The change appears to be an accidental copy-paste or find-replace spillover from Phase 8 App Token UI work.
findings.evidence:
In `crates/bodhi/src/routes/tokens/new/index.tsx`:
- Line 22: `{ label: 'New API Token', current: true }` (breadcrumb, unchanged)
- Line 40: `<CardTitle>New App Token</CardTitle>` (changed in this diff)

The git diff `4dea5ea9..HEAD -- crates/bodhi/src/routes/tokens/new/index.tsx` confirms the CardTitle was deliberately changed from `New API Token` to `New App Token` while the breadcrumb constant was left as `'New API Token'`. The same page now presents two different labels for what is the same route and action.
findings.verify_notes: This is a real defect introduced in this diff. The `/tokens/new/` route creates user-issued API tokens (programmatic access); the new `/tokens/apps/` route manages third-party app grants. Renaming the CardTitle to "New App Token" on the API token creation page creates a direct contradiction with the breadcrumb on the same page and confuses it with the distinct App Tokens feature. Priority "important" is correct — it harms the information architecture of a security-sensitive feature where users must understand which token type they are creating.

findings.title: grantItems.ts (grantableModelItems/grantableMcpItems) has no unit tests
findings.issue: The shared bucketing logic in grantItems.ts — which maps AliasResponse variants to AccessItem grant IDs — has no test file. It is the single source of truth for what models/MCPs appear in the picker and under which type group. Edge cases like ModelRouterResponse classification (see F16), duplicate ID deduplication (Map), ApiAliasResponse with null prefix, and empty arrays are untested.
findings.failure_scenario: The ModelRouterResponse misclassification bug (F16) exists in production with no failing test to catch it. A regression in ApiAliasResponse ID construction (prefix + model.id) silently breaks grants for all users creating API-model-scoped tokens.
findings.recommendation: Add src/lib/grantItems.test.ts covering: all four AliasResponse variant kinds; ModelRouterResponse mapped to a no-type or neutral-type item; duplicate IDs deduplicated; ApiAliasResponse with null prefix; empty MCP list; MCP item ID = name mapping.
findings.rationale: grantItems.ts is called from both TokenForm and the review consent screen. A regression here silently breaks grants for all users on both surfaces. It is a high-leverage shared function.
findings.evidence: grantItems.ts confirmed as a new file in the diff (git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD shows it at new file mode 100644). grep over all *.test.* files in crates/bodhi/src finds zero references to grantableModelItems or grantableMcpItems. The consumer component test files (TokenForm.test.tsx, review/index.test.tsx) exercise only ModelAliasResponse fixtures via mockModelsDefault() → createMockModelAlias(). ModelRouterResponse type (from ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts) has an `alias` field but no `models`/`prefix` fields, so it silently falls into the `else if ('alias' in alias)` branch and is classified as type:'local' — a real bug that would be caught by a unit test covering the ModelRouterResponse variant.
findings.verify_notes: The finding is accurate on all counts: the file is new, has no unit tests, and the structural narrowing logic (checking 'models' in alias && 'prefix' in alias vs 'alias' in alias) silently misclassifies ModelRouterResponse as a local model grant. The component-level tests use only ModelAliasResponse mocks, leaving ApiAliasResponse (null-prefix edge case, prefix concatenation), ModelRouterResponse classification, duplicate-ID deduplication, and empty-array paths all untested. Priority "important" is correct — this is new shared utility code on two grant surfaces, not a test gap on legacy untouched code.

findings.title: Consent approval payload test does not assert the mcps_list field
findings.location: lines 1056-1069, 'approve payload carries the model + MCP grants' assertion block
findings.issue: The test asserts models_list (line 1064), models_access (line 1066), mcps[0].status (line 1067), and mcps_access (line 1069) in the captured PUT body. It does NOT assert body.approved.mcps_list. The fixture mockDraftWithGrantFlagsResponse includes mcps_list: true in the requested flags; the review-list-mcps-toggle is rendered and clicked at line 1004. The resulting mcps_list value in the approved payload is never verified.
findings.failure_scenario: If toggleListMcps() in the consent review component had a bug that set mcps_list=false in the approved body even after the toggle is checked on, the test at line 1046 (clicking review-list-models-toggle) would succeed, the MCP select + approve flow would succeed, and the assertion block would pass — because mcps_list is absent from the assertions.
findings.recommendation: Extend the assertion block to include expect(body.approved.mcps_list).toBe(true). Add a companion test where mcps_list is toggled OFF and verify the payload carries mcps_list: false. Mirror the existing models_list assertion pattern.
findings.rationale: Both list toggles are symmetric UI controls. Asserting models_list but not mcps_list creates an asymmetric coverage hole where a regression on the MCP listing toggle would slip through the unit test layer entirely.
findings.evidence: In `crates/bodhi/src/routes/apps/access-requests/review/index.test.tsx`, the "approve payload carries the model + MCP grants" test (line 1023): (1) never clicks `review-list-mcps-toggle` — only `review-list-models-toggle` is clicked at line 1046; (2) the TypeScript cast at lines 1056–1062 omits `mcps_list`; (3) assertions at lines 1064–1069 cover `models_list`, `models_access.type`, `mcps[0].status`, and `mcps_access`, but there is no assertion on `body.approved.mcps_list`. A grep over all `*.test.*` files confirms zero occurrences of `mcps_list` in any unit test. The fixture `mockDraftWithGrantFlagsResponse` (apps.ts:456) has `mcps_list: true` in `requested`. The component at index.tsx:275 computes `mcps_list: req.mcps_list ? listMcps : false`; since the toggle is never clicked, the test silently sends `mcps_list: false` and never verifies it. The E2E test in `app-tokens-grants.spec.mjs:71` exercises `listMcps: true` via `approveWithGrants` but only asserts model-access reflection, not MCP list.
findings.verify_notes: The finding's description of "clicked at line 1004" is inaccurate — line 1004 belongs to a separate test that only asserts the element is present in the DOM, not a click. The payload test never clicks the MCP list toggle at all, meaning the coverage gap is actually worse than described: not only is `mcps_list` unasserted, but the toggled-on case is never exercised in the unit test at all. Priority stays "important" because `models_list` and `mcps_list` are symmetric controls; the missing assertion is a real coverage hole where a regression on `toggleListMcps()` or its state wiring at index.tsx:275 would go undetected at the unit layer.

findings.title: E2E spec missing positive enforcement paths — granted model infers, granted MCP connects, /v1/models + /v1/mcps reflect toggles
findings.location: line 31 — the single test covers only the no-model grant → 403 negative path
findings.issue: Phase 9 of the plan explicitly required: 'granted model infers, other model 403; granted MCP connects, other 403; /v1/models & /v1/mcps reflect toggles'. The implemented spec approves a grant of zero models and zero MCPs, then only asserts a blanket 403 for any model. It never: (a) grants a specific model and verifies that model infers successfully while a different one 403s; (b) exercises MCP grant/connect/403 at all; (c) calls /v1/models or /v1/mcps to verify the listing-filter reflection. A bug where AccessPolicy::of(ExternalApp) always returns Unrestricted would pass this spec unchanged.
findings.failure_scenario: A regression where AccessPolicy::Grants(ExternalApp) reverts to AccessPolicy::Unrestricted passes the existing spec unchanged, because the spec only tests the all-denied path (empty grant → 403). The core business value — grants allow AND deny correctly — is unverified at E2E.
findings.recommendation: Add a second scenario or extend the existing test with steps: approve a grant of one specific model + one specific MCP with list_models=true, list_mcps=true. Then assert: GET /v1/models returns only the granted model; GET /v1/mcps returns only the granted MCP; POST /v1/chat/completions with the granted model returns 200 (not 403); POST /v1/chat/completions with a different model returns 403; MCP proxy to the granted instance returns success; MCP proxy to a different instance returns 403.
findings.rationale: The plan Phase 9 gate explicitly listed positive-path enforcement as a required coverage item. Without it, regressions in AccessPolicy for ExternalApp are entirely undetected at the E2E layer.
findings.evidence: Read app-tokens-grants.spec.mjs (new in diff): grants zero models via `modelsSpecific: true`, asserts 403 at line 94-101 (negative inference), checks /bodhi/v1/user reflection (line 89-91), and verifies revoke evicts cache (line 125-135). No positive inference step, no MCP enforcement, no /v1/models or /v1/mcps listing checks. Read test_token_grants.rs (also new in diff): `external_app_grants_enforce_like_a_token` (lines 121-145) constructs AuthContext::ExternalApp with ApprovedResources granting model "a" and MCP "x9", then asserts: `ensure_model_inference("a")` → Ok; `ensure_model_inference("b")` → ModelForbidden; `ensure_mcp_connect("x9")` → Ok; `ensure_mcp_connect("y")` → McpForbidden. token_grants.rs lines 57-59 confirm Unrestricted => Ok(()), Grants(_) with no match => Err(ModelForbidden), so if policy returned Unrestricted the line-94 test would get 200 not 403 and FAIL.
findings.verify_notes: The stated failure scenario is factually incorrect. AccessPolicy::Unrestricted maps to Ok(()) for ensure_model_inference (full access), so a regression reverting ExternalApp to Unrestricted would produce 200 on the inference call, not 403 — the existing test at line 94 expects 403 and would catch it. The real coverage gap (positive inference path and all MCP enforcement untested at E2E) is genuine but is fully mitigated by the in-diff unit test external_app_grants_enforce_like_a_token which exercises ExternalApp+ApprovedResources for both positive and negative model and MCP paths. Downgraded from important to nice-to-have because the logic under test is protected at unit level and the E2E negative path remains a useful smoke check.

findings.title: AccessRequestReviewPage.approveWithGrants() has no mcpsSpecific path — owner-extra MCP picker entirely unexercised in E2E
findings.issue: approveWithGrants accepts listModels/modelsSpecific/listMcps but has no mcpsSpecific parameter. The review page renders the owner-extra MCP picker when mcps_access=true (testIdPrefix='review-mcp-access': review-mcp-access-mode-specific, review-mcp-access-panel-*, review-mcp-access-panel-done). No E2E test ever touches these selectors. Without it, the mcps_extra field in ApprovedResourcesV1 is never tested E2E.
findings.failure_scenario: A bug in the consent screen where clicking review-mcp-access-mode-specific opens an empty panel, or where review-mcp-access-panel-done submits the wrong field name (e.g. a copy-paste error mapping mcps_extra to models), would be completely undetected. The spec calls approveWithGrants with mcps_access: true in the requested scope, but the picker defaults to All without any E2E exercising the Specific path.
findings.recommendation: Add a setMcpAccessSpecific() method mirroring setModelAccessSpecific(): click review-mcp-access-mode-specific, wait for panel to open, select an MCP, click Done, wait for review-mcp-access-panel to reach state:'hidden'. Add an mcpsSpecific option to approveWithGrants and use it in a test that exercises the full owner-extra MCP grant path end-to-end.
findings.rationale: The plan explicitly requires the MCP grant path. The page object gap is the direct reason no E2E test covers mcps_extra.

findings.title: reducedMotion:'reduce' set too late — inside 'List behaviors' step, missing coverage for the preceding 'Scoped grants' step
findings.location: line 137 (inside 'List behaviors' step); 'Scoped grants' step starts at line 106
findings.issue: page.emulateMedia({ reducedMotion: 'reduce' }) is first applied at line 137 inside 'List behaviors'. The preceding 'Scoped grants' step (lines 106-133) calls selectSpecificFromPanel twice in sequence and openTokenRail — all operations that open/close Sheet overlays or trigger view-transition animations. The model-access Sheet close animation is still running when selectSpecificFromPanel('mcp-access') attempts to click the Specific radio, and the rail view-transition is live during the chip assertions.
findings.failure_scenario: On a browser with CSS animations enabled (the default before emulateMedia is called): (1) the model-access panel's Sheet close animation intercepts the click when the MCP-access Specific radio is targeted — causing a misfire or timeout. (2) openTokenRail triggers a view-transition; if the rail detail pane is still cross-fading, expectModelGrantChip asserts on a partially-mounted rail and fails intermittently.
findings.recommendation: Move page.emulateMedia({ reducedMotion: 'reduce' }) to the very top of the outer test body (before the first test.step), so it covers all steps including 'Scoped grants'. Also add a panel-close wait in selectSpecificFromPanel after clicking accessPanelDone: await this.page.locator(this.selectors.accessPanel(prefix)).waitFor({ state: 'hidden' }) mirroring AccessRequestReviewPage.setModelAccessSpecific().
findings.rationale: The emulateMedia call is scoped too late. Both the Sheet-close interception risk and the rail view-transition race are live during 'Scoped grants' but are never suppressed for that step.
findings.evidence:
In api-tokens.spec.mjs, `page.emulateMedia({ reducedMotion: 'reduce' })` appears exactly once, at line 137, inside the 'List behaviors' step. The preceding 'Scoped grants' step (lines 106-133) is fully unprotected.

Two concrete gaps in TokensPage.mjs:
1. `selectSpecificFromPanel` (lines 127-143) clicks `accessPanelDone` at line 141 then returns immediately with no `waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })` on the panel. `createTokenWithGrants` calls it for `model-access` then `mcp-access` back-to-back (lines 163-164), so the model-access Sheet slide-out animation is potentially live when the mcp-access Specific radio is clicked.
2. `openTokenRail` (lines 176-181) only calls `waitForSelector(detailRail)` to confirm DOM presence; no view-transition settle wait. `expectModelGrantChip`/`expectMcpGrantChip` assert immediately after, while the rail cross-fade can still be in progress.

All other V2 page objects (`ModelsListPageV2.mjs:62`, `SettingsPage.mjs:30`, `AllUsersPage.mjs:35`, `ChatPage.mjs:74`, `LocalDiscoveryPage.mjs:52`) call `emulateMedia` in their `navigate()` method, covering all steps. `app-tokens-grants.spec.mjs` places it at line 35, before any step. Only `api-tokens.spec.mjs` defers it to the 4th step, leaving 'Scoped grants' unprotected.
findings.verify_notes: The finding is fully confirmed. Both described race conditions are structurally present: (1) no panel-close wait in selectSpecificFromPanel after clicking done, creating a Sheet-close animation race for the sequential model-access → mcp-access picker flow; and (2) no view-transition settle after openTokenRail, creating a chip assertion race. The fix is twofold: move emulateMedia to before the first test.step, and add `await this.page.locator(this.selectors.accessPanel(prefix)).waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })` after the done click in selectSpecificFromPanel. Priority "important" is correct — these cause intermittent CI flakiness, not systematic failures.

findings.title: selectSpecificFromPanel does not wait for the picker Sheet overlay to detach before the next interaction
findings.issue: AccessPickerPanel is a shadcn Sheet with a fixed inset-0 z-50 overlay that animates out on close. After clicking Done, selectSpecificFromPanel returns immediately; createTokenWithGrants then opens the second (MCP) picker or clicks the scope card or Generate while the closing overlay may still intercept clicks. The sibling AccessRequestReviewPage.setModelAccessSpecific() correctly waits for '...-panel' to reach state:'hidden'; the TokensPage version omits that wait.
findings.failure_scenario: After closing the model-access panel, the overlay is still fading out when the code tries to click the MCP-access Specific radio. The click is intercepted by the invisible overlay, causing a misfire or element-not-interactable error. This is especially likely without reducedMotion active (see F24).
findings.recommendation: After clicking accessPanelDone(prefix), add: await this.page.locator(this.selectors.accessPanel(prefix)).waitFor({ state: 'hidden' }) before returning, mirroring setModelAccessSpecific in AccessRequestReviewPage.
findings.rationale: Avoids overlay-intercept flakiness — the same class of Sheet-detach race the consent review page already guards against.
findings.evidence: In `TokensPage.mjs` line 141-142, `selectSpecificFromPanel()` clicks the Done button and returns immediately with no subsequent `waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })` on the panel locator. In contrast, `AccessRequestReviewPage.mjs` line 60 (newly added in this diff) explicitly adds `await this.page.locator('[data-testid="review-model-access-panel"]').waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })` after clicking Done, with the explicit comment "Wait for the Sheet overlay to detach so it no longer intercepts clicks." `createTokenWithGrants()` at lines 163-164 calls `selectSpecificFromPanel('model-access')` then immediately `selectSpecificFromPanel('mcp-access')` when both flags are true, so the model-access Sheet can still be animating closed when the code tries to click `mcp-access-mode-specific`. Both files are new code in the `4dea5ea9..HEAD` diff — not pre-existing.
findings.verify_notes: The gap is a straightforward omission: the same Sheet-detach guard was correctly written in `AccessRequestReviewPage.setModelAccessSpecific()` (same diff) but not carried into `TokensPage.selectSpecificFromPanel()`. Fix: after line 141 (`accessPanelDone` click), add `await this.page.locator(this.selectors.accessPanel(prefix)).waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })` before the return. Priority remains "important" — this causes E2E test flakiness (not a data/security issue) but is a real, reproducible race that will trigger on any CI run without `reducedMotion: 'reduce'` active.

findings.title: Grant display paths fail-open (all-access) when grants JSON is unparseable, inconsistent with auth middleware's fail-closed behavior
findings.location: From<TokenEntity> for TokenDetail, line 192; also crates/routes_app/src/apps/apps_api_schemas.rs AppAccessSummary::from_row line 104
findings.issue: Two display-path read functions use fail-open fallbacks when grants JSON is corrupt. (1) TokenDetail::from: serde_json::from_str(&t.grants).unwrap_or_default() falls back to TokenGrants::default() (list_models=true, models=All, list_mcps=true, mcps=All) when the stored grants column cannot be parsed. The auth middleware (token_service.rs) introduced in this diff does the opposite: it fails closed with TokenError::InvalidToken on a corrupt grants column. (2) AppAccessSummary::from_row: uses .and_then(|json| serde_json::from_str::<ApprovedResources>(json).ok()) with no warning log, swallowing deserialization errors silently. This was also the failure mode for tokens created with McpGrant::None (serialized as {type:"none"}) which was removed in this diff with no DB migration: the management UI displays such rows as all-access while every auth attempt is rejected 401.
findings.failure_scenario: For (1): A token's grants column is corrupted. GET /tokens/{id} returns grants:{list_models:true, models:{type:'all'}, ...} — all-access. The user sees no indication of a problem. Every API call using that token returns 401 (auth middleware fails closed). User sees a token that looks fine but never works. For (2): An invalid approved JSON blob appears in the DB. AppAccessSummary shows the grant as Approved with empty model+MCP access — indistinguishable from a legitimately empty-grant record. No log entry alerts on-call.
findings.recommendation: For TokenDetail::from: change the fallback to TokenGrants::V1(TokenGrantsV1 { list_models: false, models: ModelGrant::Specific { ids: [] }, list_mcps: false, mcps: McpGrant::Specific { ids: [] } }) (empty-deny) so the display is conservative and hints at a problem. For AppAccessSummary::from_row: add a tracing::warn! inside the and_then closure when deserialization fails, logging the row id and raw JSON length, before calling .ok(). For the McpGrant::None migration: add a SeaORM migration converting grants column values containing "type":"none" to "type":"specific","ids":[].
findings.rationale: Display and authentication paths give opposite answers for the same corrupt row. While the auth path correctly refuses access, the display path can mislead users into thinking the token is fully functional, delaying diagnosis of a corruption issue.
findings.sources: sec:enforcement F5 (McpGrant::None removal), bug:services (TokenDetail), sec:lifecycle F7 (AppAccessSummary)
findings.evidence: 1) crates/services/src/tokens/token_objs.rs lines 190-192: comment literally says "fall back to all-access defensively rather than panicking on an unexpected payload" and code is `serde_json::from_str(&t.grants).unwrap_or_default()`. TokenGrants::default() (lines 108-117) is V1 { list_models: true, models: All, list_mcps: true, mcps: All } — full all-access. 2) crates/routes_app/src/middleware/token_service/token_service.rs lines 135-138: comment says "Fail closed: a corrupt grants payload rejects the token rather than silently granting all-access" and code is `serde_json::from_str::<TokenGrants>(&api_token.grants).map_err(|e| TokenError::InvalidToken(format!("Invalid grants: {}", e)))?` — opposite behavior. 3) McpGrant::None introduced at 4dea5ea9 (diff start), removed at f1265209 without a DB migration; any token row with grants containing {"type":"none"} fails both parsers: auth returns 401 (fail-closed), display returns all-access (fail-open). 4) AppAccessSummary::from_row in crates/routes_app/src/apps/apps_api_schemas.rs lines 97-118 (new code in diff): parse failure arm returns Specific { list: false, ids: [] } (empty-deny, not all-access), with no tracing::warn! call.
findings.verify_notes: The asymmetry is real and ironically self-documented: the two code paths have opposing explanatory comments. However, this is a diagnostic/UX defect, not a security bypass — the auth middleware correctly rejects corrupt-grants tokens, so no unauthorized access occurs. The McpGrant::None migration concern is bounded to the development phase (introduced and removed within this same feature branch/range before any production deployment); existing production rows cannot have that variant. AppAccessSummary's failure path is correctly deny-empty (not all-access), so part (2) of the finding is slightly overstated in impact. Priority nice-to-have is appropriate: the recommendation to change unwrap_or_default() to an empty-deny fallback and add a warn! log are valid quality improvements with no security urgency.

findings.title: delete_token service-level 404 mapping is untested
findings.issue: The new method's whole reason to exist is the existence pre-check that maps a missing row to EntityError::NotFound("Token") (404) rather than letting the repository's DbError::ItemNotFound surface as a 500. Repository-level delete is tested (test_delete_api_token, test_cross_tenant_token_delete_blocked) but the service-level mapping branch is not — there is no test asserting delete_token on a missing id yields the NotFound domain error via .code().
findings.failure_scenario: A regression changes the error mapping in delete_token to propagate ItemNotFound as-is. GET /tokens/{id} DELETE on a non-existent token returns 500 instead of 404. No service-level test would detect this.
findings.recommendation: Add a test_token_service.rs case: delete_token of an unknown id returns the not-found TokenServiceError/EntityError (assert via .code()); happy path deletes and returns Ok(()).
findings.rationale: The mapping is the only logic the method adds; without a test the 404-vs-500 contract can regress unnoticed.
findings.evidence: The `delete_token` method was introduced in this diff at `crates/services/src/tokens/token_service.rs` lines 222-244. Its only logic beyond delegating to the repository is the existence pre-check: `.ok_or_else(|| crate::EntityError::NotFound("Token".to_string()))`. The diff for `test_token_service.rs` adds only `grants: Default::default()` to an existing test — no `delete_token` test was added. Reading the full `test_token_service.rs` (286 lines) confirms there is no test that exercises `delete_token` at any path. The repository-level test at `test_token_repository.rs:338` verifies `DbError::ItemNotFound` for missing rows, but that is the layer below the service-level 404 mapping, which remains uncovered.
findings.verify_notes: The gap is real: if the `ok_or_else` branch were removed or changed to re-propagate `DbError::ItemNotFound` as-is, no service-level test would catch the 404→500 regression. Priority stays nice-to-have (not critical) because the method is simple, the mapping is a single expression, and Phase-9 E2E tests likely exercise the 404 path indirectly at the HTTP level.

findings.title: New cache test uses wrong (actual, expected) assertion order
findings.issue: assert_eq!(cache.get("exchanged_token:aaa"), None) puts actual first, expected second, contrary to the crate convention assert_eq!(expected, actual). The pre-existing test above it has the same shape (consistency drift rather than a newly introduced violation in isolation), but the new test added in this diff follows the wrong order.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — convention only. On assertion failure, pretty_assertions diffs display 'expected' and 'actual' columns swapped, making the diff harder to read.
findings.recommendation: Flip to assert_eq!(None, cache.get(...)). The remove_entries_containing logic and matrix coverage are otherwise correct.
findings.rationale: Minor but the convention exists so pretty_assertions diffs read correctly.
findings.evidence: The diff adds two assert_eq! calls in `test_remove_entries_containing` (crates/services/src/utils/cache_service.rs lines 101-102): `assert_eq!(cache.get("exchanged_token:aaa"), None)` and `assert_eq!(cache.get("exchanged_token:bbb"), None)` — actual-first, expected-second. The dominant convention across the services crate is expected-first: grep -rn "assert_eq!(None," shows 15+ usages in auth_context.rs, test_api_alias_repository.rs, test_settings_repository.rs, test_session_service.rs, etc. The pre-existing test at line 78 also uses actual-first (pre-existing drift), but the two newly-introduced lines in this diff consistently follow the wrong order.
findings.verify_notes: Confirmed as a real convention violation introduced in this diff. The logic and coverage of the test are correct; only the argument order to assert_eq! is reversed relative to the `assert_eq!(expected, actual)` crate convention. On failure, pretty_assertions will show "left"/"right" swapped, making the failure message misleading. Priority remains nice-to-have — no functional defect, no test correctness impact.
