findings.title: Cache eviction needle relies on undocumented compact-JSON serialization format — silent eviction failure on format change
findings.issue: access_request_cache_needle produces "access_request_id":"<id>" — a raw substring of the compact serde_json serialization of CachedExchangeResult. The eviction call in routes_apps.rs invokes remove_entries_containing(&needle). Correctness depends on three undocumented assumptions: (1) CachedExchangeResult is always serialized compact (no spaces); (2) the JSON field name is exactly access_request_id (no rename/alias); (3) the field value is a plain string (no escaping needed for ULID characters). If any future struct change breaks the match, revocations silently stop being immediate — no compile-time or test-time signal.
findings.failure_scenario: A developer renames the access_request_id field in CachedExchangeResult to ar_id. All subsequent revocations appear to succeed (HTTP 200, DB status = Revoked) but the cache is never evicted. The external app's previously-cached token continues to work for up to 5 minutes after revocation. The failure is invisible — no error, no test failure.
findings.recommendation: Add a unit test that serializes a CachedExchangeResult with a known access_request_id value and asserts .contains(access_request_cache_needle(id)) on the result. This ties the needle to the actual serialized form and catches any rename or format change at CI time.
findings.rationale: Revocation immediacy is the sole mechanism preventing a revoked ExternalApp from accessing model-inference endpoints until TTL. If eviction silently stops working, revoked tokens keep working for up to 5 minutes with no indication.
findings.sources: sec:lifecycle F8, prior:cross-cutting-review F5, prior:routes_app-review F5
findings.evidence:
Checked the actual source:

1. `/crates/routes_app/src/middleware/token_service/token_service.rs` lines 22–47: `CachedExchangeResult` has `pub access_request_id: Option<String>` with no `#[serde(rename)]` and no struct-level `#[serde(rename_all)]`. The needle function at line 45 hardcodes `format!("\"access_request_id\":\"{}\"", access_request_id)`.

2. Line 213 caches the struct via `serde_json::to_string(&cached_result)` — standard compact serialization, which correctly matches the needle today.

3. `/crates/routes_app/src/apps/routes_apps.rs` lines 471–474 call `access_request_cache_needle(&id)` then `remove_entries_containing(&needle)`.

4. `/crates/services/src/utils/cache_service.rs` lines 82–105: The `test_remove_entries_containing` test hardcodes the needle string `r#""access_request_id":"ar-1""#` as a raw literal, NOT via `access_request_cache_needle()`. It tests the eviction mechanism in isolation but does NOT tie the needle generator to the struct serialization.

5. `/crates/routes_app/src/middleware/token_service/test_token_service.rs`: grep confirms zero references to `access_request_cache_needle` — no test serializes a `CachedExchangeResult` and asserts the needle is a substring of the result.

6. `/crates/routes_app/src/apps/test_access_request.rs` (revocation tests): tests verify DB status change and list exclusion after revoke, but no assertion on cache state or `remove_entries_containing` being called.

The gap is real: no test ties the needle string to the actual serialized form of `CachedExchangeResult`. A field rename or a `#[serde(rename)]` annotation would silently break revocation immediacy with no CI failure.
findings.verify_notes: The current code works correctly — compact serde_json serialization, no rename attrs, and ULID characters are alphanumeric (no escaping needed). The code also has an explicit doc comment acknowledging the assumption ("Assumes CachedExchangeResult is serialized with serde_json's compact form... Co-located with the struct so it tracks the serialized field name; revisit if the cache format changes."). The doc comment provides some signal but is not machine-checked. The sole fix needed is a unit test that calls `serde_json::to_string(&cached_result)` and asserts `.contains(access_request_cache_needle(id))`, which would catch any future field rename or serde attribute addition at CI time. Priority nice-to-have is correct: this is a maintainability/test-coverage gap on a currently-working mechanism, not a present defect.

findings.title: Collection endpoint /bodhi/v1/access-requests/apps placed inside the per-ID action namespace
findings.issue: All other paths under /bodhi/v1/access-requests/ are per-resource ID actions: /{id}/review, /{id}/approve, /{id}/deny, /{id}/revoke. The new collection endpoint for listing issued app grants uses the literal path segment 'apps' in the same namespace, mixing a collection route with per-resource routes. This makes 'apps' look visually like an {id} value, and GET /access-requests/apps/revoke would route to the revoke handler with id='apps' (returning 404 from DB, not 405). The parallel API token collection lives at /bodhi/v1/tokens (a top-level resource).
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — design/convention issue. A developer exploring the spec expects /access-requests/{id} to be the only dynamic segment and is surprised to find a static 'apps' sibling that is semantically a collection endpoint, not an ID action.
findings.recommendation: Move the collection to a cleaner namespace: /bodhi/v1/apps/tokens (parallel to /bodhi/v1/tokens for API tokens) or /bodhi/v1/apps/grants. This separates the management collection from the per-request lifecycle actions and aligns with the existing /bodhi/v1/apps/* external-app namespace.
findings.rationale: The /access-requests/ namespace is established for lifecycle actions on specific requests. A collection endpoint in the same namespace violates the established pattern.
findings.evidence: Confirmed in crates/routes_app/src/apps/routes_apps.rs lines 21-25: the namespace `/bodhi/v1/access-requests/` contains four per-resource routes `/{id}/review`, `/{id}/approve`, `/{id}/deny`, `/{id}/revoke` plus the new static collection at `/access-requests/apps` (line 24). The revoke handler is registered as `post(apps_revoke_access_request)` (routes.rs line 364). The stated failure scenario is inaccurate: the finding claims "GET /access-requests/apps/revoke returns 404 not 405", but since revoke is a POST handler, a GET to that path would correctly return 405. A POST /access-requests/apps/revoke WOULD match the revoke handler with id='apps' and return DB 404 — but this is the corrected form of the finding's concern.
findings.verify_notes: The design inconsistency is real: `/bodhi/v1/access-requests/apps` (static collection) is a sibling of `/{id}/*` routes in the same namespace, which is non-standard compared to the `/bodhi/v1/tokens` top-level pattern. However, the specific failure scenario in the finding is factually wrong — it asserts a GET request would yield 404 instead of 405, but the revoke handler is POST, so GET /access-requests/apps/revoke does return 405 correctly. The finding is plausible (not confirmed) because the stated concrete failure is erroneous. Priority stays nice-to-have: purely a convention/readability concern with no security or correctness impact.

findings.title: OperationId 'approveAppsAccessRequest' uses plural 'Apps' breaking the naming pattern of sibling operations
findings.issue: The owner-facing action endpoints use inconsistent operationId naming: createAccessRequest, getAccessRequestStatus, getAccessRequestReview — no 'Apps' qualifier; approveAppsAccessRequest — adds plural 'Apps'; denyAccessRequest — no qualifier; listAppAccess, revokeAppAccess — singular 'App', different suffix style. Auto-generated TypeScript client method names will be approveAppsAccessRequest() vs denyAccessRequest(), which is confusing for client authors writing access-request lifecycle code.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — convention only. Auto-generated TS SDK exposes approveAppsAccessRequest() vs denyAccessRequest(), and client authors must remember that approve has 'Apps' in the method name but deny does not.
findings.recommendation: Rename to approveAccessRequest (drop 'Apps') to match denyAccessRequest; or rename all consistently: createAppAccessRequest, getAppAccessRequestStatus, getAppAccessRequestReview, approveAppAccessRequest, denyAppAccessRequest. Also align listAppAccess and revokeAppAccess to the chosen convention.
findings.rationale: SDK auto-generation derives method names from operationId. Inconsistent casing and qualifiers produce an SDK where callers must memorize per-operation naming variations.
findings.evidence: grep on routes_apps.rs confirms all seven operation IDs currently in source: createAccessRequest, getAccessRequestStatus, getAccessRequestReview, approveAppsAccessRequest, denyAccessRequest, listAppAccess, revokeAppAccess. `git show 4dea5ea9:...routes_apps.rs | grep operation_id` shows the first five were already present at the base commit unchanged. The diff (4dea5ea9..HEAD) adds only two lines: `operation_id = "listAppAccess"` and `operation_id = "revokeAppAccess"`.
findings.verify_notes: The primary example named in the finding title — approveAppsAccessRequest with its plural "Apps" — is pre-existing and unchanged in this diff, which weakens the finding's applicability to the reviewed commits. The two newly added IDs (listAppAccess, revokeAppAccess) do add to the naming inconsistency (singular "App" qualifier vs no qualifier in older sibling IDs), but this is a minor secondary point. The inconsistency is real in the current codebase, so "plausible" (rather than "refuted") is appropriate; priority stays nice-to-have as this is a convention-only cosmetic issue affecting auto-generated SDK method names.

findings.title: AccessPickerPanel group labels 'Local Models' and 'API Models' hardcoded regardless of noun prop
findings.issue: When items carry a type field, the slide-in panel groups them under the hardcoded labels 'Local Models' and 'API Models', ignoring the noun prop. The component is designed for sharing between models and MCPs (comment: 'Shared by models and MCPs'), but contains model-specific strings. Currently MCP items have no type so hasTypes is false and the labels are never shown for MCPs. Risk materialises when typed non-model items are added.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — architecture/convention issue. Risk triggers only when typed non-model items are added in the future.
findings.recommendation: Derive group labels from noun: 'Local ${noun}s' / 'API ${noun}s', or accept optional localGroupLabel/apiGroupLabel props defaulting to the current strings.
findings.rationale: The component is explicitly designed for sharing but contains model-specific strings, creating a maintenance trap for future resource types.
findings.evidence: AccessPickerPanel.tsx is a new file in this diff (git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD shows `new file mode 100644`). Lines 56-57 read: `if (local.length) out.push({ label: 'Local Models', items: local });` and `if (api.length) out.push({ label: 'API Models', items: api });` — both hardcoded, ignoring the `noun` prop. Every other user-visible string in the component uses `noun`: line 52 (`${noun}s`), line 76 (`Search ${noun}s…`), line 99 (`No {noun}s match…`), line 132-133 (`{noun} selected`). The parent component AccessPicker.tsx line 29-30 has the JSDoc: "Shared by models and MCPs." TokenForm.tsx confirms both callers: `noun="model"` (line 151) and `noun="MCP"` (line 174). The latent-only safeguard is confirmed: `hasTypes = items.some((i) => i.type)` (line 40) is false for MCP items today (they carry no type field), so the hardcoded labels are never rendered currently.
findings.verify_notes: The finding's description of the failure mechanism is accurate. The component is explicitly designed for sharing between models and MCPs (parent's JSDoc) but bakes in model-specific group label strings rather than deriving from the noun prop. No current user-visible impact since MCP AccessItem objects have no type, keeping hasTypes false and the grouped code path unreachable for MCPs. Risk is latent and surfaces only when typed non-model items are introduced. Priority nice-to-have is correctly rated.

findings.title: ReviewContent fetches models and MCPs unconditionally even when the app requested neither grant type
findings.issue: useListModels and useListMcps are always called at component mount regardless of whether the access request includes models_access, models_list, mcps_access, or mcps_list flags. React-Query rules prevent conditional hook calls, but the enabled option could be set to false when the flags are absent. Two extra API calls fire for every consent review even if the GrantBlock sections are never rendered.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — correctness issue. The excess calls succeed silently and the data is unused. The GrantBlock sections only render when the flags are set, so the populated modelItems/mcpItems are discarded.
findings.recommendation: Set enabled based on reviewData flags: useListModels(1, 100, 'alias', 'asc', undefined, { enabled: !!(reviewData && (req?.models_access || req?.models_list)) }). This requires waiting for reviewData before enabling, which React-Query supports natively.
findings.rationale: Aligns data fetching with actual usage — only load the picker datasets when the review screen will actually show the pickers.

findings.title: AppDetailPanel hardcodes 'active' status chip instead of reading app.status
findings.issue: AppDetailPanel renders active unconditionally. The AppAccessSummary type has a status field with possible values 'draft' | 'approved' | 'denied' | 'failed' | 'expired' | 'revoked'. The hardcoded text and CSS class ignore the actual value. The analogous TokenDetailPanel in routes/tokens/index.tsx correctly reads token.status from data.
findings.failure_scenario: Currently no runtime impact because list_approved_for_user filters to Status=Approved only. However if a revoked app appears in the list — e.g. through a cache-staleness window after a local state update, or if the backend adds support for showing recently-revoked items — the chip will always display 'active' regardless of app.status.
findings.recommendation: Replace the hardcoded chip with: const isApproved = app.status === 'approved'; {app.status}
findings.rationale: AppAccessSummary.status is available and should be used. Hardcoding 'active' is a latent bug if the invariant that the list only contains approved items is ever relaxed.
findings.evidence: git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD shows the line was introduced in this diff: `+ active` at AppDetailPanel in crates/bodhi/src/routes/tokens/apps/index.tsx line 254. AppAccessSummary.status is typed as 'draft' | 'approved' | 'denied' | 'failed' | 'expired' | 'revoked' (ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts line 245) and is on the app prop. The backend list_approved_for_user (services/src/app_access_requests/access_request_repository.rs line 365) filters to Status=Approved only, so no runtime user impact today. The analogous TokenDetailPanel in routes/tokens/index.tsx lines 351/359 correctly reads token.status dynamically.
findings.verify_notes: Confirmed real defect introduced in this diff. The backend invariant (only Approved items returned) prevents user-visible impact currently, keeping priority at nice-to-have. The fix is trivial: replace the hardcoded chip with `const isApproved = app.status === 'approved'` and `{app.status}`, mirroring TokenDetailPanel. Test fixtures already include mockAppAccessRevoked (test-fixtures/apps.ts line 38) demonstrating the type supports non-approved statuses in frontend test data.

findings.title: ListingToggle keyboard activation (Space/Enter) not unit-tested
findings.location: line 25 — only click toggling is tested
findings.issue: ListingToggle.tsx lines 41-47 implement an onKeyDown handler that fires onToggle for Space and Enter keys. The component is rendered as a div with role=checkbox and tabIndex=0, so keyboard interaction is the primary accessible path. The test file has 4 tests (render, click-toggle, redundant-hint, disabled-click-no-fire) but none covers keyboard activation.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — convention. The onKeyDown handler fires onToggle on Space/Enter when not disabled; if this code path regressed (e.g. key check changed from 'Space' to ' ' or vice versa) no unit test would detect it.
findings.recommendation: Add two unit tests: userEvent.keyboard(' ') on the toggle element → onToggle called; userEvent.keyboard('{Enter}') → onToggle called. Also add a disabled variant asserting neither key fires onToggle.
findings.rationale: The component explicitly advertises keyboard accessibility (role=checkbox, tabIndex=0). Testing only click paths leaves the keyboard handler untested.
findings.evidence: Both files are new in the diff (4dea5ea9..HEAD). ListingToggle.tsx lines 41-47 implement onKeyDown that fires onToggle for e.key === ' ' or e.key === 'Enter' when not disabled. The test file has exactly 4 tests (render, click-toggle, redundant-hint, disabled-click-no-fire); all interaction tests use userEvent.click only. There is no userEvent.keyboard or fireEvent.keyDown call anywhere in the test file.
findings.verify_notes: The keyboard handler code is correct as written (e.key === ' ' is the right DOM value for Space). The gap is purely test coverage: a future refactor of the key check (e.g. to 'Space' or 'Spacebar') would silently break accessibility without any test catching it. Priority nice-to-have is appropriate — this is a coverage gap for an a11y code path, not a functional bug in the shipped code.

findings.title: AccessPickerPanel type filter (Local/API dropdown) not unit-tested
findings.location: line 74 — search filter is tested; type filter select is not
findings.issue: AccessPickerPanel.tsx lines 38-48 implement a typeFilter state and a <select> (data-testid='{prefix}-panel-type') that appears when any item has a type property. The ITEMS fixture in the test has mixed types (local: llama3:8b, mistral:7b; api: gpt-4o). The panel-type select is rendered but never exercised in the tests.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — convention. If typeFilter filtering logic regressed (e.g. swapped local/api labels), no unit test would catch it. The model token form renders the dropdown in production but the unit harness never selects from it.
findings.recommendation: Add a test that opens the panel, selects 'Local' from the type filter select, and verifies only local items appear (llama3:8b and mistral:7b visible; gpt-4o hidden). Add a companion test for the 'API' filter.
findings.rationale: The type filter is the primary UX differentiator between the model picker (mixes local+API models) and the MCP picker (no types). It is new code in this diff with no coverage.
findings.evidence: AccessPicker.test.tsx is a new file in this diff (git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD confirms new file). It has 5 tests covering mode rendering, panel open, item select/remove, search filter, and count display. None of the tests interact with `model-access-panel-type`. AccessPickerPanel.tsx lines 38-48 implement `typeFilter` state and lines 82-93 render `<select data-testid="{prefix}-panel-type">` with All/Local/API options, conditional on `hasTypes`. The ITEMS fixture in the test (llama3:8b type=local, gpt-4o type=api, mistral:7b type=local) makes `hasTypes=true` for every test that opens the panel, so the select IS rendered — it's just never exercised. The filter predicate at line 45 (`if (hasTypes && typeFilter !== 'all' && i.type !== typeFilter) return false`) has no test coverage.
findings.verify_notes: The gap is real and in new code. The type filter is a primary UX differentiator for the model token form (mixing local+API models) vs. the MCP picker (no types). A regression swapping 'local'/'api' labels or breaking the filter predicate would pass the entire unit suite. Priority nice-to-have is correct — this is a missing coverage case on working code, not a production defect.

findings.title: GrantBlock component has no dedicated unit test
findings.issue: GrantBlock.tsx (new in this diff) is a composition wrapper around ListingToggle + AccessPicker that supports showListing=false and showAccess=false to selectively render each half — the consent screen uses these to show only the controls the app requested. There is no GrantBlock.test.tsx. The showListing/showAccess props are exercised only indirectly through the consent screen unit test (review/index.test.tsx).
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — convention. If showListing=false accidentally still rendered the ListingToggle (e.g. condition inverted), the consent screen test might still pass if it only checks for the presence of the wrapper testid and not the absence of the toggle.
findings.recommendation: Add GrantBlock.test.tsx covering: renders both halves by default; showListing=false hides the listing toggle but shows the picker; showAccess=false shows the toggle but hides the picker; disabled=true passes disabled to both child components. Keep it shallow (no MSW needed).
findings.rationale: GrantBlock is the shared seam between two critical screens (token form and consent review). Testing its conditional rendering contract directly makes the component resilient to future props changes.
findings.evidence: No GrantBlock.test.tsx exists in crates/bodhi/src/components/access-picker/ (only AccessPicker.test.tsx and ListingToggle.test.tsx are present). The consent review test (index.test.tsx) uses mockDraftWithGrantFlagsResponse which sets all four flags (models_list, models_access, mcps_list, mcps_access) to true, so only the showListing=true + showAccess=true combination is exercised through GrantBlock. The 'omits the grant sections' test uses mockDraftReviewResponse which prevents GrantBlock from rendering at all. No test exercises showListing=false with showAccess=true or vice versa — the mixed partial combinations that are the component's primary design purpose (consent screen shows only the controls the app requested).
findings.verify_notes: The conditional rendering logic in GrantBlock (lines 66 and 79) is simple JSX short-circuit guards. An inversion of the all-true path would be caught by the existing consent screen test (line 1001 asserts review-list-models-toggle is present). The uncaught gap is the partial combinations (showListing=false, showAccess=true etc.) which the consent screen always passes as derived from req.models_list/req.models_access — and no fixture supplies a mixed partial case. Priority nice-to-have is correctly rated: the bug risk is low given the trivial logic, but the direct unit test for the component's conditional-render contract is missing.

findings.title: TokenForm scope_token_power_user card disabled state not tested for resource_user role
findings.issue: TokenForm.tsx:83 computes canPowerUser from userInfo.role and sets the Power User role card disabled when the user is resource_user (line 202). The test's beforeEach mounts mockUserLoggedIn({}, { stub: true }) which returns the default role. There is no test verifying that when userInfo.role is 'resource_user', the scope_token_power_user card has disabled=true and cannot be clicked to change scope.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — convention. If the canPowerUser guard regressed (e.g. condition always true), a resource_user could create a scope_token_power_user token via the UI. No unit test would detect this client-side elevation.
findings.recommendation: Add a test using mockUserLoggedIn({ role: 'resource_user' }) that asserts scope-card-scope_token_power_user has attribute disabled and clicking it does not change the selected scope. Add a complementary test for resource_power_user where both cards are enabled.
findings.rationale: The canPowerUser guard is a client-side enforcement of a role boundary. Unit-testing the client guard ensures regressions are caught at the fastest feedback level.
findings.evidence: TokenForm.tsx:83 computes `canPowerUser = userInfo?.auth_status === 'logged_in' && userInfo.role !== 'resource_user'`. TokenForm.tsx:202 uses `disabledCard = card.scope === 'scope_token_power_user' && !canPowerUser` and sets `disabled={disabledCard}` on the button (line 211). In TokenForm.test.tsx:106, the shared `beforeEach` calls `mockUserLoggedIn({}, { stub: true })`, which via user.ts:35 defaults `role` to `null` — making `canPowerUser` true in every rendered test. Searching the entire test file for `resource_user`, `resource_power_user`, `canPowerUser`, and `disabled` finds zero matches related to the scope card. The only appearance of `scope_token_power_user` in the test file is line 81, inside a `toCreateTokenRequest` data-transform unit test that does not render the component. No test mounts the form with `role: 'resource_user'` to assert the power-user card carries `disabled=true` and ignores clicks.
findings.verify_notes: The production guard is correctly implemented; this is purely a test coverage gap. The client-side guard is UX-only — the backend enforces the role boundary (the server rejects a `scope_token_power_user` token created by a `resource_user` caller), so a regression in this guard would not be a security issue. Priority "nice-to-have" is correctly rated.

findings.title: Phase 4 'List-all with All access' second E2E case not implemented
findings.issue: Phase 4 specifies a second case: 'List-all toggles on with All access.' The spec only exercises Specific mode (with listModels:true), never the mode='all' picker branch, and never toggles MCP list-all. The complementary case — listModels=true + mode=All (no Specific panel), listMcps=true + mode=All — is never exercised E2E, leaving the 'All' mode radio cards and their interaction with the detail rail summary untested end-to-end.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — coverage gap. A regression where the 'All' access mode radio card for models is visually broken or not persisted to the token payload would not be caught by E2E.
findings.recommendation: Add a second createTokenWithGrants call with listModels=true, listMcps=true and no specificModels/specificMcps flags (mode=All by default). After creation, open the token rail and assert it shows 'All models' / 'All MCPs' summary text rather than specific chips.
findings.rationale: The plan explicitly required this second case. Without it, only the Specific branch of the AccessPicker is covered E2E.
findings.evidence: grep -rn "createTokenWithGrants" across tests-js/ returns exactly two hits: the method definition in TokensPage.mjs and a single call site at api-tokens.spec.mjs:115 with { listModels: true, specificModels: true, specificMcps: true }. The "All" branch (listModels=true, specificModels=false) is never invoked. TokensPage.mjs has an accessModeSpecific selector but no accessModeAll selector, and no test calls selectSpecificFromPanel with the alternative (All) path omitted on purpose. The git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD confirms the entire "Scoped grants" step is new to this diff and was written with only the Specific mode path.
findings.verify_notes: This is a real coverage gap, not a misread of the code. The "All" access-mode radio card for both models and MCPs is never exercised E2E; only the Specific panel flow is tested. Priority nice-to-have is correct — it is purely a test coverage gap with no confirmed functional defect, and a regression in the "All" radio card rendering or persistence would be invisible to the E2E suite.

findings.title: Keyboard-nav logic inlined in spec using internal .l-listrow.active CSS class selector
findings.issue: The spec directly constructs the locator using the internal CSS class .l-listrow.active: '${tokensPage.selectors.tokensTable} .l-listrow.active [data-testid="row-link"]'. This leaks CatalogRow's active-class convention into the spec. The mellow-porcupine plan explicitly required a page-object method selectRowByKeyboard(dir) for keyboard nav. The .l-listrow CSS class is a private shell implementation detail.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — naming/architecture issue. If the CSS class name changes or the link moves inside the row, the spec breaks while the page object continues to compile.
findings.recommendation: Add a focusActiveRowLink() method (or selectRowByKeyboard(dir)) to TokensPage that encapsulates the compound locator. The spec then calls tokensPage.focusActiveRowLink() before keyboard navigation.
findings.rationale: Plan required a page-object method. CSS class coupling makes the spec harder to maintain when the list component structure changes.
findings.evidence: Line 148 of `api-tokens.spec.mjs` (new addition in this diff) directly uses `.locator(\`${tokensPage.selectors.tokensTable} .l-listrow.active [data-testid="row-link"]\`)`. `TokensPage.mjs` has no `focusActiveRowLink()` or `selectRowByKeyboard()` method — only `selectors.tokensTable` is abstracted. The `.l-listrow` class is assigned inside `CatalogRow` at `crates/bodhi/src/routes/models/explore/-shared/catalog-table.tsx:91` (`className={\`l-listrow cat-row${active ? ' active' : ''}\`}`) — an internal shell CSS convention, not a stable `data-testid`. A CSS rename would break this locator while the page object compiled fine.
findings.verify_notes: Finding is architecturally accurate and the defect is real: the spec couples directly to an internal CSS class rather than delegating to a page-object method. Priority "nice-to-have" is correct — the test passes today and `.l-listrow` is a stable-ish convention used across multiple list screens, so the breakage risk is low but real. The fix is straightforward: add a `focusActiveRowLink()` helper to `TokensPage` that encapsulates the compound selector.

findings.title: INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER env vars not validated in beforeAll — silent failure in standalone project
findings.location: lines 259-262 (manager credentials inline); beforeAll validation at lines 21-25 does not cover them
findings.issue: The multi-user test builds managerCredentials with process.env.INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER and INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER_ID without any guard. The describe-level beforeAll validates only INTEG_TEST_USERNAME / _PASSWORD / _USERNAME_ID (via getTestCredentials) and apiKey. If the manager env vars are absent, the test fails at LoginPage.performOAuthLogin() with an obscure OAuth error rather than a loud 'Missing INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER' message. Convention: 'Never test.skip() for missing env vars; throw in beforeAll so failures are loud.'
findings.failure_scenario: CI environment with INTEG_TEST_USERNAME set but INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER absent: beforeAll passes (admin creds valid), standalone test runs, managerCredentials.username is undefined, LoginPage fails at the username fill step with a Playwright 'value must be a string' internal error. Root cause is invisible from the error message.
findings.recommendation: In beforeAll (or in a guard at the top of the multi-user test body before the try block), throw if INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER or INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER_ID is falsy: if (!process.env.INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER) throw new Error('Missing INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER for multi-user test')
findings.rationale: Convention requires throwing in beforeAll for missing env vars so that CI surfaces the configuration gap with a useful message rather than a confusing downstream failure.
findings.evidence: File is in the diff (4dea5ea9..HEAD). The `beforeAll` at lines 21-25 calls `getTestCredentials()` (which throws for missing `INTEG_TEST_USERNAME`/`_PASSWORD`/`_USERNAME_ID`) and `getAuthServerConfig()` (which throws for missing auth URL vars), but performs no validation of `INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER` or `INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER_ID`. At lines 259-263, `managerCredentials` is built inline from `process.env.INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER` (→ `username`) and `process.env.INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER_ID` (→ `userId`) with no null check. The `auth-server-client.mjs` file shows the established pattern — `getTestCredentials()`, `getRealmAdminCredentials()`, `getMultiTenantConfig()` all throw explicitly for missing vars — but no equivalent helper exists or is called for the manager user. If `INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER` is absent, `managerCredentials.username` is `undefined`, which is passed to `LoginPage.performOAuthLogin()` and will fail with an obscure Playwright error rather than a clear configuration message.
findings.verify_notes: The finding is accurate and the code gap is real. Both missing env vars (`INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER`, `INTEG_TEST_USER_MANAGER_ID`) are consumed inline without any guard in `beforeAll` or in the step body before the `try` block. The project convention (documented in memory as `feedback_no_skip_for_missing_env.md`) explicitly requires throwing in `beforeAll` for missing env vars. Priority stays at nice-to-have: this is a test DX issue only — no production code is affected, the test still fails (just with a confusing message), and the manager env vars are likely always set in the integration CI environment where this test runs.

findings.title: TokenGrantsV1 uses verb-first field names (list_models, list_mcps, models, mcps) diverging from the domain-first convention applied to app envelopes
findings.issue: Commit 8844c135 (part of this diff) renamed the app approval fields to domain-first order: models_list, models_access, mcps_list, mcps_access in both RequestedResourcesV1 and ApprovedResourcesV1. TokenGrantsV1 was left with the old pre-rename names (list_models, models, list_mcps, mcps). Both implement ResourceGrants and appear together in the OpenAPI schema. The code comment acknowledges 'intentionally standalone', but the divergence is now on a naming axis (ordering convention) rather than a structural one, making cross-reference confusing in documentation and generated SDKs.
findings.failure_scenario: N/A — API schema asymmetry only. A developer or API consumer comparing TokenDetail.grants schema against ApproveAccessRequest.approved schema in the OpenAPI spec sees inconsistent field naming for equivalent concepts: list_models vs models_list, models vs models_access.
findings.recommendation: If the rename is intended as a project-wide convention, align TokenGrantsV1 to models_list/models_access/mcps_list/mcps_access and provide a DB migration updating stored JSON in the api_tokens.grants column. If the divergence is intentional and permanent, document it explicitly in the TokenGrantsV1 struct comment with a cross-walk table so future contributors understand the mapping.
findings.rationale: The inconsistency was introduced as a side-effect of applying the domain-first rename only to the app approval side. Since both schemas appear in the same OpenAPI spec and represent semantically parallel constructs, the naming divergence is a discoverability hazard for SDK and documentation consumers.
findings.sources: api:typechain F11, arch:backend F18, prior:cross-cutting-review F1
findings.evidence:
Confirmed the asymmetry in current source:

`TokenGrantsV1` (crates/services/src/tokens/token_objs.rs, lines 34-43):
- `pub list_models: bool`
- `pub models: ModelGrant`
- `pub list_mcps: bool`
- `pub mcps: McpGrant`

`ApprovedResourcesV1` (crates/services/src/app_access_requests/access_request_objs.rs, lines 256-268):
- `pub models_list: bool`
- `pub models_access: ModelGrant`
- `pub mcps_list: bool`
- `pub mcps_access: McpGrant`

Commit 8844c135 renamed all four fields in `ApprovedResourcesV1` (list_models→models_list, models→models_access, list_mcps→mcps_list, mcps_extra→mcps_access) but left `token_objs.rs` entirely untouched (git show 8844c135 -- crates/services/src/tokens/token_objs.rs produced no diff).

Mitigating evidence: The commit message for 8844c135 explicitly states "TokenGrantsV1 (the separate API-token type) keeps its existing list_models/list_mcps naming." The struct comment at lines 31-32 reads "Intentionally standalone — NOT shared with the App-access-request envelope (`ApprovedResources`); the two may diverge." The divergence is therefore a documented, intentional design decision, not an overlooked side-effect.
findings.verify_notes: The naming asymmetry is real and will be visible in the OpenAPI spec to SDK and documentation consumers — the finding is factually accurate. However, the finding characterizes it as an unintentional "side-effect," whereas the commit message and the struct-level doc comment both explicitly acknowledge the divergence as intentional. This falls into the "documented accepted design decision" category rather than an unnoticed bug. Priority nice-to-have is correct: there is a legitimate discoverability concern for API consumers, but no functional breakage, and a future PR aligning the names would also need a DB migration for any stored TokenGrantsV1 JSON (the migration crate already seeds the old wire format at m20250101_000025_token_grants.rs line 12).

findings.title: access_request_auth_middleware ApiToken bypass has no unit test — regression risk for new MCP-proxy ApiToken path
findings.location: entire file — no ApiToken test case exists despite the middleware explicitly adding AuthContext::ApiToken to the Session bypass arm at access_request_middleware.rs line 92
findings.issue: The middleware now contains `AuthContext::ApiToken { .. } => AccessRequestAuthFlow::Session` (line 92 in the changed middleware) that explicitly bypasses access-request lifecycle validation for API tokens. The test file covers Session passing through (test_mcp_session_auth_passes_through) and MultiTenantSession passing through (test_mcp_multi_tenant_session_passes_through) but has zero tests for the ApiToken case. If the `ApiToken` arm is accidentally removed — for example during a merge that rebases the three-arm `Session | MultiTenantSession | ApiToken` pattern back to `Session | MultiTenantSession` — ApiToken would hit the wildcard `_` arm and receive `Err(MissingAuth)` (403) instead of passing through. This is a regression risk for the newly enabled ApiToken→MCP proxy flow: the diff simultaneously added `Some(TokenScope::User)` to the apps_apis `api_auth_middleware` call (routes.rs line 435) so ApiToken can now reach the apps_mcp_exec route group, making the middleware bypass load-bearing.
findings.failure_scenario: Remove `| AuthContext::ApiToken { .. }` from the three-arm bypass in access_request_auth_middleware.rs. An ApiToken that previously reached `/bodhi/v1/apps/mcps/{id}/mcp` now receives 403 MissingAuth. No existing test catches this regression: all test cases use Session, MultiTenantSession, or ExternalApp auth contexts.
findings.recommendation: Add a test in test_access_request_middleware.rs similar to test_mcp_session_auth_passes_through but using `AuthContext::test_api_token("user", TokenScope::User)`. Route a request through the middleware and assert 200, confirming the ApiToken arm is exercised.
findings.rationale: The middleware's ApiToken bypass is a new invariant introduced in this diff, required by the intentional design decision to let ApiToken reach MCP proxy routes. Without a unit test, the invariant is undocumented in code and can silently regress.
findings.evidence: 1. `crates/routes_app/src/middleware/access_requests/access_request_middleware.rs` line 92 (in the diff, confirmed present in HEAD): `| AuthContext::ApiToken { .. } => AccessRequestAuthFlow::Session` — the bypass arm is real and newly added by this diff. 2. Searching the test file for "ApiToken" returns zero hits; the four test functions are `test_mcp_session_auth_passes_through`, `test_mcp_multi_tenant_session_passes_through`, `test_mcp_oauth_lifecycle_validation`, `test_mcp_oauth_app_client_mismatch_forbidden` — none exercises the ApiToken path. 3. The `routes.rs` diff confirms `Some(TokenScope::User)` replaced `None` in the `api_auth_middleware` call for the `apps_mcp_exec` router (line ~432), meaning `AuthContext::ApiToken` with `TokenScope::User` now reaches this middleware and the bypass arm is load-bearing. All three pieces of evidence are present in the committed code.
findings.verify_notes: The finding is accurate. The `ApiToken` bypass is a new invariant introduced by this diff, it is load-bearing (ApiToken now reaches the middleware thanks to the TokenScope::User change in routes.rs), and the test file has zero coverage for it. Silently removing the `| AuthContext::ApiToken { .. }` arm would cause 403 MissingAuth for all ApiToken MCP-proxy requests with no test catching the regression. Priority stays "important" — not "critical" because the middleware is covered for the Session/MultiTenantSession paths (the common case) and ApiToken MCP-proxy is a new feature path, but the gap is a real regression risk for a design invariant that was explicitly added in this diff.

findings.title: app_mcps() union of by-url approved instances and owner-extra IDs is untested with both sources populated
findings.location: ResourceAccess::app_mcps(), lines 81-101 — the McpGrant::Specific branch collects by-url approved instances then appends owner-extra ids
findings.issue: ResourceAccess::app_mcps() builds the effective MCP list by unioning two sources: (1) by-url approved instances from `grants.mcps` where `status == ApprovalStatus::Approved`, and (2) owner-extra IDs from `grants.mcps_access` (McpGrant::Specific). The deduplication uses a Vec::contains check. The test in test_user_info.rs (test_user_info_handler_external_app_reflects_grants) uses `mcps: vec![]` with `mcps_access: Specific { ids: ["mcp-1"] }` — only the owner-extra source is populated. No test exercises the union path where both `mcps` (with at least one Approved instance) and `mcps_access.ids` are non-empty, including the deduplication invariant when the same ID appears in both lists.
findings.failure_scenario: Set `mcps = [{ url: ..., status: Approved, instance: { id: 'shared-mcp' } }]` and `mcps_access = Specific { ids: ['shared-mcp', 'extra-mcp'] }`. The expected output is `ids: ['shared-mcp', 'extra-mcp']` (deduplicated). A bug that removes the `contains` guard would produce `ids: ['shared-mcp', 'shared-mcp', 'extra-mcp']`. No existing test catches the duplicate.
findings.recommendation: Add a unit test for ResourceAccess::app_mcps() in test_user_info.rs or a dedicated users_api_schemas test module with a fixture that populates both mcps (approved by-url) and mcps_access.ids and asserts the union is correct and deduplicated.
findings.rationale: The union and deduplication logic is non-trivial and is the only place that computes the effective reflected MCP set for the /app/user endpoint. Correctness at this layer is observable by clients and should be directly tested.
findings.evidence: The `ResourceAccess::app_mcps()` function (crates/routes_app/src/users/users_api_schemas.rs, lines 81-104) is entirely new code introduced in this diff. The union logic at lines 87-96 first collects by-url approved instances (`grants.mcps.iter().filter(status == Approved)`), then appends `mcps_access.ids` only if not already present (`Vec::contains` guard at line 94). The sole integration test `test_user_info_handler_external_app_reflects_grants` (test_user_info.rs line 540) sets `mcps: vec![]` (empty — no by-url instances) and `mcps_access: McpGrant::Specific { ids: vec!["mcp-1"] }`. A grep across all `test*.rs` files in `crates/routes_app/src/` confirms no test populates both `mcps` (with at least one `ApprovalStatus::Approved` entry having a non-None `instance`) and `mcps_access.ids` simultaneously. The deduplication invariant (lines 93-96) is therefore never exercised.
findings.verify_notes: This is a test-coverage gap in newly committed logic, not a runtime bug — the dedup code itself appears correct. The priority remains nice-to-have: the production path is straightforward `Vec::contains` deduplication with no conditional branching that could regress silently. A unit test directly on `ResourceAccess::app_mcps()` with a fixture that has overlapping IDs in both sources would close the gap efficiently without needing a full HTTP handler round-trip.

findings.title: approved_role is validated against user's Keycloak resource role at exchange time but not at list time — a DB-tampered approved_role survives in AppAccessSummary
findings.issue: token_service.rs lines 376-401 validate that `approved_role` in the DB does not exceed the user's current Keycloak resource role at token-exchange time. AppAccessSummary::from_row() (used by both GET /access-requests/apps list and POST /access-requests/{id}/revoke) parses `approved_role` directly from the DB without the same privilege-escalation check. If a DB row is tampered to hold an `approved_role` of `scope_user_power_user` for a user whose Keycloak role is `resource_user`, the list and revoke response will reflect the elevated role even though token exchange would reject it. This is a display-layer inconsistency, not an enforcement bypass, but it could mislead an owner reviewing their own app grants.
findings.failure_scenario: Tamper with an AppAccessRequest row to set approved_role = 'scope_user_power_user' for a user whose KC role is User. GET /access-requests/apps returns AppAccessSummary with approved_role: power_user. The actual token cannot be exchanged at power_user level (token_service rejects it), but the UI shows the elevated grant, confusing the owner.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) apply the same privilege-ceiling check in from_row — cap approved_role at the user's current resource role — or (b) document in the API spec that approved_role in the list endpoint reflects the stored value (which may have been capped at exchange time), so clients should not rely on it for security decisions.
findings.rationale: The token-exchange validation and the list-display path diverge on privilege capping. While not an enforcement gap, the inconsistency is a latent confusion vector when investigating a revoked or tampered grant.
findings.verify_notes: The divergence is real and confirmed. However, the priority "nice-to-have" is correct and should not be raised. Exploitation requires DB-level write access (an attacker who can modify rows can do far worse than confuse a display); enforcement at token-exchange time is not affected — a tampered `approved_role` would be rejected at exchange with `PrivilegeEscalation`. The only consequence is display confusion in the list/revoke response body shown to an app owner. No security bypass exists.

refuted.title: AccessPolicy::of pierces version wrapper via .v1() defeating the versioning abstraction
refuted.verify_notes: The architectural observation is valid: `ResourceGrants` is currently only implemented on `TokenGrantsV1` and `ApprovedResourcesV1`, not on the versioned wrapper enums `TokenGrants` / `ApprovedResources`. Implementing the trait on the wrappers would make `AccessPolicy::of` version-agnostic and is a cleaner design. However, the stated risk — "silent Unrestricted access for V2 tokens" — cannot materialise because Rust's exhaustive pattern matching in `.v1()` guarantees a compile-time failure if any new variant is added without updating those methods. This is a refactoring/style suggestion (nice-to-have), not a correctness or security defect.

refuted.title: copyTokenFromDialog() uses page.evaluate() to read clipboard and a naked waitForTimeout(100)
refuted.evidence: git show 4dea5ea9:crates/lib_bodhiserver/tests-js/pages/TokensPage.mjs confirms that copyTokenFromDialog() with both waitForTimeout(100) (line 136) and page.evaluate(() => window.clipboardData) (line 137) existed verbatim at the base commit. The diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD for that file (commit 5125255a) only adds new methods (selectScope, setListAll, selectSpecificFromPanel, grant chips) and does NOT touch copyTokenFromDialog() at all. git diff 4dea5ea9..HEAD -- crates/lib_bodhiserver/tests-js/pages/TokensPage.mjs produces no matches for copyTokenFromDialog, waitForTimeout, or clipboardData.
refuted.verify_notes: The defects described (naked waitForTimeout and page.evaluate for clipboard) are real anti-patterns per the E2E conventions in tests-js/CLAUDE.md, but they are pre-existing/unchanged in this diff and therefore fall outside the scope of the current review range. The finding should not be attributed to the commits under review. If the team wants to fix this, it belongs in a separate cleanup task, not as a finding against this diff.

refuted.evidence: git show 4dea5ea9:crates/lib_bodhiserver/tests-js/pages/TokensPage.mjs | sed -n '215,222p' shows the function body identical to HEAD: `await token.statusSwitch.click(); await this.waitForSPAReady(); await this.page.waitForTimeout(100);`. refuted.verify_notes: The convention violation (waitForTimeout banned by tests-js/CLAUDE.md) is real, but it predates the current review window. Per review rules: "pre-existing/unchanged in this diff" → refuted. If this is to be fixed it belongs in a separate tech-debt commit, not as a finding against this diff. Priority downgraded to nice-to-have since the issue is a test-code style violation on a stable, passing test path, not a production correctness defect.

refuted.title: Cache eviction side-effect in revoke handler not verified by integration test
refuted.evidence: The E2E test at crates/lib_bodhiserver/tests-js/specs/tokens/app-tokens-grants.spec.mjs lines 125-135 has an explicit test.step "Revoked immediately: the previously-working token is now rejected". It (1) pre-seeds the cache by having the app exchange a token earlier in the test (the token is cached), (2) calls revokeAccess() via the UI (which hits the revoke handler), and (3) immediately re-uses the same token and asserts auth_status === 'logged_out'. The step comment reads: "Revoke evicts the cached token-exchange result, so the very next call with the same token is rejected (no 5-minute TTL wait)." Deleting remove_entries_containing from routes_apps.rs:474 would leave the exchange result in the 5-minute Moka cache, causing that step to return auth_status of an authenticated user instead of 'logged_out', and the test would FAIL. The unit test in test_access_request.rs does lack the cache-seed assertion, but the E2E test provides exactly the integration coverage the finding says is missing.
refuted.verify_notes: The finding scoped its review to crates/routes_app/src/apps/test_access_request.rs and concluded the gap exists at all layers. However, the E2E suite in crates/lib_bodhiserver/tests-js/specs/tokens/app-tokens-grants.spec.mjs covers the precise failure scenario (immediate token rejection after revoke). A unit-level integration test in test_access_request.rs that seeds the cache and asserts eviction would still be a nice-to-have for faster feedback and isolation, but the claim that "If the remove_entries_containing call were deleted, the test would still pass" is incorrect given the E2E coverage.

refuted.title: Inference handlers return 403 (echoing model id) while listing returns 404 — inconsistent existence oracle
refuted.evidence: In routes_oai_chat.rs line 133-134, `ensure_model_inference` is called BEFORE `find_alias` (the existence check). The function in token_grants.rs returns `ModelForbidden` (403) based solely on the grant list, not on model existence. The comment documents this explicitly: "a non-granted model must 403, not leak existence via 404." A scoped token calling POST /v1/chat/completions for a model named `secret-model` or `unicorn-model-xyz-that-doesnt-exist` both receive identical 403 responses — the 403 cannot distinguish "exists but not granted" from "doesn't exist at all."
refuted.verify_notes: The finding claims "receives 403 … now knows the model is addressable," but this is incorrect. The 403 fires for any model name not in the token's grant list, regardless of existence. The design deliberately uses 403 at inference to avoid leaking existence information (which a 404 would provide if the check were ordered differently). The listed `GET /v1/models/{id}` endpoint also checks listability before doing a DB lookup, following the same principle. The surface-level 403-vs-404 asymmetry is a deliberate and correct design that prevents existence oracle attacks via inference — not a vulnerability or inconsistency.

refuted.title: findTokenByName uses nameText.includes(name) — can silently return the wrong row when token names share a prefix
refuted.evidence:
1. The `|| nameText.includes(name)` fallback at `/crates/lib_bodhiserver/tests-js/pages/TokensPage.mjs:283` was already present in commit `4dea5ea9` (confirmed by `git show 4dea5ea9:…TokensPage.mjs | grep includes` returning line 181 with the identical condition). The diff from `4dea5ea9..HEAD` adds zero new lines containing `includes` in TokensPage.mjs — the only new line is an added `findTokenByName` call at line 129.
2. All current callers pass exact fixture-name strings: `tokenNames.admin1` = `'Admin Token 1'`, `tokenNames.admin2` = `'Admin Token 2'`, `tokenNames.basic` = `'Test API Token'`, etc. (confirmed by grepping `api-tokens.spec.mjs`). The exact-equality branch (`nameText === name`) always matches for these strings, so the `includes` branch is never reached.
3. The failure scenario (calling `findTokenByName('Admin Token')` as a common prefix) does not occur anywhere in the test suite. `TokenFixtures.getTestTokenNames()` returns only complete distinct names, none of which is a prefix of another.
refuted.verify_notes: The `includes` fallback is a genuine code smell and a latent risk if future tests search by a substring. However it is (a) pre-existing, unchanged by this diff, and (b) never triggered by any current test call, so no active defect exists in the code under review. Priority remains nice-to-have — the fix (remove the `includes` fallback, use strict equality only) is low-effort cleanup worth doing at leisure but not blocking any release.
